Huge shoutout to the Age of Empires IV dev team — absolutely masterful work. You've managed to lock me out of online play for three days straight. I've tried literally everything: restarting the game, restarting my PC, verifying game files, even spinning up a VPN node in Seoul... nothing. The game still dumps me into offline mode the moment I launch it, even though my internet is 100% rock solid.
700+ hours in this game btw, so I'm not just some random tourist. At this point, I genuinely hope you keep up this momentum. Do the entire RTS community a favour and make this game completely disappear from the scene — that would honestly be your greatest achievement. Legends. 👏

Update: Servers are back, so as is tradition, I’m flipping my review back to positive. Good job, devs. Seriously, keep it up — like, actually keep the servers alive this time. I have the most unhinged love-hate relationship with this team. ❤️🤬 See you on the ladder… until the next mental breakdown.

225080264.review:
This game is THE premier RTS available to fans of the genre right now. The diversity of civilizations and gameplay styles is amazing. The attention to detail on the history of (almost) every civ and variant is commendable. The music is immersive, and now that I've finally upgraded to an overkill desktop after playing this on my laptop for 3+ years, I can confidently say the graphics are beautiful in 4k. 

The balance for multiplayer is tweaked every couple months and while never perfect, the devs are clearly committed to taking in feedback and making positive changes to support as many players as possible. If you like turtling and teching up, greedily booming, or full baboon all-in attacks, this game has a civ you can learn and find success with. 

If you're like me, and 30+ and miss the heyday of Starcraft, Warcraft 3, and other classics that we never realized we're so lucky to be inundated with, I think this is the best way to scratch the RTS itch and improve in a deeply complex and rewarding game. It's not a perfect game, but I can't recommend it enough.

225032462.review:
This is one of the most rewarding multiplayer experiences you can get anywhere. Age of Empires IV is the newest entry in the legendary AOE franchise. It features several high-production campaigns and a roguelike game mode for single-player gamers, but the real treat is multiplayer. Once you start reaching higher leagues, you need to be constantly reacting to your opponent to counter their strategy and pull out a win. It's basically a game of chess, but both players are playing simultaneously. If you want a more casual experience, Nomad FFA is one of the most chaotic and fun game modes.

What makes AOE4 better than other RTS's I have played? For one, the production design is incredible. Every civilization has unique voice lines, music and architecture, and the sound design is the best in the industry. What sets the game even more apart is the base-building aspect of it. In this game, you don't always win by producing the most units the fastest, like in StarCraft. You can also build up defenses to build up your eco and win in the long run.

Unfortunately, the game does suffer from balance issues with some of the new DLC factions, and the developers have been a bit slow in fixing bugs and communicating with their player base. For a lot of people, the variant civilizations are a major downside, but in my opinion, they provide enough of a unique experience to the game that I don't mind their inclusion.

Without those issues, this game is a 10 out of 10 for me, and I can highly recommend it if you are looking for a fun competitive multiplayer to get into.

225022656.review: There is a profound, almost primal satisfaction in booting up AoE4. At its heart, the experience feels remarkably like being a child playing with his toy soldiers on the living room rug—meticulously lining up rows of cavalry, hiding archers behind makeshift plastic walls, and crashing them together in a glorious, chaotic mess. Only this time, this is a sprawling, historically rich medieval landscape, and the stakes feel incredibly real.

225015750.review:
This game has an incredible amount of potential. I've put a couple of hours into this game here and there and after all of this time i can confidently say that the only thing holding this Game back is its management. A lack of QoL and hands on active development as well as a certain "tone deafness" to feedback from its community dampens what could otherwise be the uncontested King of RTS, which could have sparked a revival in the RTS genre that competitors so far have failed to achieve.

Do i recommend this game? Yes and no. If you are looking for a game with active development and a management that is listening to the feedback of its players and has an actual feelable passion for the game, you should probably try Age of Empires 2 instead.

If you are looking for a modern RTS with incredible gameplay and can overlook bad decisions on a weekly basis, or if you just enjoy fighting on social media in regards to poor developer/management decisions, then this game has plenty of content for that!

A game that wishes it was Age of Empires 2 where you also have to buy the Civilizations that were in Age of Empires 2. Also playing with mods seems to disable achievements(Still, in this day and age, this garbage restriction. Even Paradox is no longer doing this). The ai is actually less enjoyable than the AI from Age of Empires 2, they just constantly attack the same spots with massive armies. Allied AI seems just as bad and toward the end of the game AI just ends up camping in their bases waiting for me to attack with my massive army then build buildings to spawn more units on their doorstep to destroy them. Even with PLENTY of resources left on the map.(At least 5 gold nodes and 4 stone nodes in a heavily forested map, some of which were in the enemy bases, ignored). It's not that great. I don't recommend it, just play AoE2. If you want innovative/different gameplay in the series, play AoE3.

This game would have been slightly better with higher unit caps, closer to 300 or 400, but with only 200, it's just AoE2 with DLC to buy Civilizations you already had in 2 with maybe a coat of paint and a few extra features that aren't worth it.

Immersion completely ruined by the game being depicted as a "history lesson". Cars and modern-day technology are all over the place in the cutscenes, and the actual battles are just sillhouettes of ghost-warriors while the narrator talks to you as if she's a History teacher telling you about "what happened here 1000 years ago". I really hate it. Age of Empires 2 depicted the cutscenes as YOU BEING THERE IN THE MOMENT, whether you were a warrior or one of Saladin's followers or whatever. And if you wanted the full history lesson, there was a section for that hidden away in the menus. Turning your video game into a History Lecture sucks!

tl;dr  the stylistic choices kill the immersion

One of my favorite RTS games of all time. I played over 150 hours on Gamepass, but couldnt justify the price leap. A nice blend of city/base building time, small conflicts, into major wars. 224162311.review: Arguably the best modern RTS at the moment. It combines the best things from all age of empires into one game. The pacing is well made, not as slow as Aoe II and not as fast as AoE III, it's just perfect. Huge variety of civilizations, mostly balanced, differences between civs is much larger than in a game like AoE II, which makes it more fresh and fun to play.  Map has RNG so it doesn't get as boring as SC2. It's very addicting game, rewarding  skill. Ranked system is great, you can play ranked 1v1, 2v2, 3v3, 4v4, after each win going against stronger and stronger opponents. Winning gives you so much dopamine, sadly losing feels awful, but you need to learn to lose and take it as lessons. Before you jump to multiplayer, I highly recommend to beat 1v1 "hardest" difficulty AI as French in Dry Arabia. This will test your basic understanding of the game and once you feel comfortable beating, you're ready for ranked and should be close to silver / gold rank.

223932942.review:
I disagree with the top review in the sense that the game is in a very good state. It is hard not to cater to pros, when the genre of RTS by default is a high APM, game knowledge, strategy game with a strong focus on being as optimized and as efficient as possible. There is a lot of ways to play alongside high skill expression and hidden mechanics. The civs are very unique and are for the most part relatively balanced considering how many civs there are and how difficult it is to balance all the civs. The game takes a long time to learn, but the journey has been enjoyable. My current review focused solely on the gameplay is a 10/10. 

HOWEVER, what sucks is when playing ranked, there is no option to reconnect. Because of this, the game is essentially over the moment a player dcs. There is no ranked points loss mitigation either. This seems to happen about 1/10 games. If devs fix this, at least players won't feel like it ruins the experience.

Overall, needs some QoL improvement over another civ, as the game is in a good state. 

Edit: There's another guy that stated "Suck ass servers, Microsoft can't afford dev to create smooth server properly. Having money but no management skills." Again adding an option to reconnect would benefit greatly.

Edit 2: Walls are in a good state right now. The only way they get insta-deleted is if you allow the game to progress into the extreme late stages of the game, where everyone and their mother has seige. Games usually end in castle age unless something was missed when scouting. Both wood and stone walls have their purpose. This game both fortunately and unfortunately has a ridiculously high learning curve, where the first 100 hours can practically be treated as a tutorial.

223817608.review:
Age of Empires IV is basically what happens when someone looks at AoE 2 and says “what if we made it less fun but more stressful?”

I’ve got hundreds of hours in AoE 1, 2, and 3. Loved every minute. Those games felt like strategy, growth, and actual back-and-forth battles.

AoE 4 gave me 13 hours of being personally victimized by the AI.

And I do mean victimized. Not “challenged.” Not “outplayed.” I’m talking about getting rolled by an AI that feels like it’s running on cocaine and ancient war knowledge while I’m still trying to remember where the villager hotkey is. I’d be peacefully building a farm and suddenly my entire civilization is getting erased by a medieval NASCAR event.

Even on lower difficulties, it’s like the AI skipped the tutorial, read a Sun Tzu audiobook, and decided my existence was optional.

The wild part is it looks like Age of Empires. It sounds like Age of Empires. But somewhere between those 13 hours of suffering and confusion, I realized I wasn’t having fun—I was just getting professionally bent over by a spreadsheet in armor.

I didn’t uninstall because I lost. I uninstalled because I started feeling like the AI was enjoying it too much.

Bad:

223000457.review:
I HIGHLY don't recommend this game if you want to play MULTIPLAYER specially solo or duo queque. Reasons:

1. No rejoin feature — getting disconnected instantly removes you from the match, which feels outdated and unfair.

2. Matchmaking inconsistency — players of very different ranks (Gold vs Bronze/Silver) are frequently matched together.

3. Poor team balance — high-level players are often grouped with much lower-level teammates against stronger opponents.

4. Level mismatch frustration — teams feel uneven, making matches one-sided and hard to win.

5. Lack of competitive fairness — imbalance reduces the impact of individual skill.

6. Team games feel unreliable — outcomes depend too much on matchmaking rather than strategy.

Overall experience — these issues make the game frustrating and less enjoyable despite its core gameplay

222919806.review:
So I haven't played much recently. I have been playing AOE for almost 25 years. I have loved every game, and every expansion. I was stoked when this was announced. But I can no longer recommend AOE4 to anyone. They are forcing every player to play like pros do instead of letting them goof around like us players have for years. 

I hop on, again after months of being away, only to find that I basically can't build walls anywhere. They were already pretty worthless in this game, along with towers (unlike previous AOE games), but at least when I want to have a fun relaxing time turtling against AIs opponents, I could waste as much time as I wanted. It wasn't about the competition for me, it was about the escape. Now there is no escape, there is "play like you have all the macros memorized or be crushed", so freaking dumb. I've seen similar changes in the AOE2 Definitive edition but have too much nostalgia for 2 to give it a poor review.

Like all the other AOE games, this one was, at one point, something relaxing and fun for me. With a nice challenge. Now its just annoying. When my walls started auto-deleting their build placement because it violates a stupid new rule that should only be in effect during a pvp game (or arguably a tournament game), I get ticked off. 

Part of the charm of this franchise is that you could play with so many different strategies and have fun doing it. Now this game seems like it is trying to edge out all the casual players (who many have played for decades like me btw and care nothing for the pro scene). Just another game Microslop is finding a way to make worse. 

Shame on Relic. Shame on Microslop. Shame on World's Edge. Shame on Forgotten Empires. 
You all forgot the first rule of a game: for players to have fun.

222381890.review:
I have always had a place in my heart for the Age of Empires series. From playing AoE2 against the AI and trying to make large armies of Teutonic knights, to starting my competitive gaming experience playing AoE3, all AoE games have always done well in my book...and AoE4 is no different.

The sheer work and dedication in trying to bring the love of history into an RTS game is no small feat - and while some people may believe that AoE2 will never lose it's place as the #1 game in the series, AoE4 makes the greatest effort to bring what we have loved from all AoE games to the modern era and does a great job to improve on past criticisms.

222363723.review:
It is very much a great middle ground between Age Of Empires II and III, taken the best of both and blended them together to make a while that is both familiar and new at the same time.

TL;DR - If you're a fan of the series and a little unsure about it, get it on sale. Its worthwhile giving it a shot. Who knows what you'll find?

Hail the return of the king... square maps. The circular ones in AoE III were a big change and, while they needed to try something new to keep it fresh, it didn't feel right, and with the addition of the home shipment system, I think it was a bit of future shock. Too much change too fast. It didn't feel like Age Of Empires. One thing I LOOOOVE with this one is being able to choose biomes for the skirmish maps, the map presentation is very, very pretty, and all the biomes are beautifully represented.

The UI takes a bit to get used to as, while the information is all there, you just need to adjust a little. Its not a bad thing, its not miles away from the well established one from the entries before, its just slightly different. One little addition I really appreciate is that when you click on a unit and put the cursor over the unit name, it gives you the readout on it's strength and weaknesses, just under that you'll find all the stats like attack damage, armour, and range.

Aging up is done by building landmarks, kinda like wonders from the previous entries, and there are two to choose from each time. Like the French have the Artillery School that creates stronger versions of the siege units built in it, or many of them have a landmark building that is a large keep (see: castle from AoE II) such as England's Berkshire Castle.

There's an AoE III style emphasis on civilisation bonuses too. Unique units as always, economic bonuses, and unique buildings like the Ottomans can create a limted amount of Military Schools from the first age which automatically generate military units, or the Delhi Sultanate (Indians) can garrison Imams units inside a building and it creates bonuses for the buildings around them.

One of my biggest bug bears with AoE II is that monks are OP (especizally the Aztecs) but in this one they've nerfed the monks in a beautifully simple way: the monks can't convert from a long distance, they need to get within (effectively) shouting distance, and they can only convert while holding a relic. 

Now, the downsides.
- The sigil (player banner) creator is piss poor. The elements in it are fine and all but there are limited colour options for each element... and they're all some sort of pastel or muted colour... and none of them work together. Nothing strong. Nothing that seemingly commands attention and authority. If you were to fight under these banners yourself... you'd not be inspired. Come on now.

- The game as a whole seems to lean a little toward multiplayer. Obviously many people WILL play online but some people like to just chill and play solo, the map selections are a little too few for my liking as well.

- The civilisations are a little too few (means they can give more as paid DLC. Yay.) and while its nice to have two or three versions of some (like there is both the English, and their subsidiary, House of Lancaster, as another varient) there really needs to be more variety. There's no Spanish or Portuguese, and their buildings would be beautiful!

- The unit stance options (or lack thereof) is really quite annoying. The units need to be stationary before you can hold ground, but after you move them you need to hit hold ground again. There is no defensive stance option. It seems a little under refined, like the sort of thing you'd see in AoE 1 when it was first released, instead of a more intelligent system like in AoE II.
I generally emphasise building attack with my siege engines and have my armies in close cupport, but considering I can't put them in a defensive stance with my ranged units holding ground, it means hold my units back manually... its a pain in the butt.

All in all though, its worth a shot. Get in on sale if you're unsure. I was unsure but it currently has it's claws in me and they're buried deep!

222335347.review:
AoE 4 is a rather nicely made game, and a good entry in the series. I find the movie segments to open up each part of the campaign is brilliant - the combination of live-action shots of locations superimposed upon by animations detailing the historical event the campaign's mission is focused on is a good way to open up with, if a little lengthy.
Overall, I find the landmark function with aging up a nice element to work with, and the variety of units is a decent balance. Campaign missions have a good pace, and compared to AoE 2, I find this a good thing to ease yourself through it, as the difficulty levels are balanced well to ensure players have fun. Definitely worth the price I paid for it.

222196430.review: There is very little to no strategy here, I actually don't get how this game has any hype at all really. The missions are all beatable with the exact same play. Do whatever the game clearly intends for you to do at the start of the mission or die, and then turtle the base until you have the resources to max out tech and spam endless units until you win. Not exactly the grand strategy i was looking to develop. You CAN micro units for better fight results to win faster, but just sending endless units always works. And as far as online goes I personally don't want RTS PvP games that end in 6 mins without it specifically being some kind of lightning round mode. AoE2/3, Dune 2000, Warcraft 3 are all kinda old games that are better than this game imo. Hell, I think the combat in Caesar 3 is better and that game is a city builder XD.  If you are looking for large scale fights with some actual strategy the Total War series is the best i've found so far. The AI is better, and is still lacking in adaptive strategy once you get your military up to size, but you as the player absolutely feel rewarded for coming up with strategies beyond just max units and full frontal assault and in most cases it will be required to come up with some kind of plan to not get wiped. I've been bored and couldn't find a game that was really calling to me so I purchased Age of Empires IV because of its high reviews, but I'm here to tell you they are wrong. While not terrible, this is not a very good game, and it's a very far cry from what I was expecting 2+ decades after the original entry. If you can get this game for $10 or less it might entertain you for a weekend, but if you are into RTS you are going to burn out fast on this one.

221597534.review:
I cannot believe that they did not learned absolutely anything from AoE3.

AoE3 is masterfully designed compared to 4, why?
It has a lot of benefit for map control, you cannot tower rush, it was a lot of build diversity and each civ can be played in hundreds of ways, resources are easy to understand, ageup macro makes sense and the map is extremely easy to read.

Counter system in AoE4 is a mess compared to AoE3, the tag and multiplier system in 3 is sooo much modern and interesting compared to 4.

Somehow civs can feel sameish bc of the unit types being universal, AoE3 solves that by having a tag system and making many different and unique units (Not to mention Mercs, Outlaws and Natives) have the same tag as their counterparts from other civs, openings and ways to open and strategize is day and night from civ to civ compared to this game.

If you want to see peak RTS design try 3 instead, its free after all. 

The game can be fun tho, but... yeah I'd stick to 3.

221546644.review:
I absolutely love this game, and remember it being nearly magical day one first release.

8/10! Mainly because the story is short. It's 10 missions long. each taking less than an hour to finish. 7.5 hours played. 

I wanted to see more timeline progress, on into things like flintlock pistols and cannons even... although I am happy with the medieval style gameplay, I also think it is lacking a lot of past units, and functions. You only have two different militant units to choose from per building as far as archery, barracks, and stables. So 6 there. 3 different siege units, and a small handful of upgrades. One or two each. Nothing serious. 

Nothing buggy to report on the campaign side, just expected more gameplay. Now it's just unending, or "special" challenges to complete or survive as long as you can.

Endless mode seems fairly moot, considering you cap at 200, you would probably obtain far more food than needed if not wood, and run out of the means to mine gold and stone, eventually wood.

I suppose there is always DLC but, I am sort of old school, and would rather purchase one game and not be pushed into buying addition content for a game I paid a fair amount for. 

Not bad, just not exciting anymore after the story is done yet so short after starting.

220208468.review:
Age of Empires 4 is the equvalent of Starcraft 2 compared Starcraft 1 in many ways. A lot of the same ideas and themes, unit counters and play styles, and familiar gameplay you know from AoE2 have been re-imagined and streamlined for more accessibility and moved to a 3d format instead of 2d. 
At the time of writing this in 2026, there is still a healthy community playing online. And while not as popular as SC2, the devs have put in the effort to add new content to the game and put out balance patches since its release in 2023. 
While the game is a little generic in some ways in terms of the gameplay and unit designs (compared to say Starcraft), the fact that there are multiple victory conditions by default, as well as random map generation by default, makes for a lot of interesting situations that can occur. And the amount of civilizations you can play does provide a lot of variety and different situations to consider.
One thing that is top notch to me is the effort the devs put into the atmosphere and sound. Despite being an RTS the sound design really brings you into the world of the game. 
Overall a solid AoE game that returns more to its roots of AoE 2 and modernizes it in a 3D format. 
8/10.

218670228.review:
Let me start by saying that this is my personal, subjective opinion. So, if you have a different experience, I respect that 100%.

On the positive side, this game probably has the best base building in any modern RTS. Each building has randomly generated assets that meld buildings together, and make late game bases look like fully functioning cities. If not for the combat, you could easily mistake this game for a city builder.

BUT, my biggest gripe with this game is the combat. 167 hours and I just can't get into it. The Rock Paper Scessors dynamic is too strong in this game, if a unit counters a unit it counters it HARD. There's no way for that unit to fight back in anyway. And that just sucks in my books.

Battles feel like garbage with units floating all over the place with no sense of weight. And speaking of Units, they are INCREDIBLY stupid and require constant baby sitting, or they'll end up focusing on the wrong targets and die horribly. Eh, it's arcadey in every sense of the word, and it is by far the weakest part of the game, for me. 

I gave it a fair shot, bought a dlc or two, tried a lot of nations, and always goes the same way, I enjoy the base building and unique economy techs of each nation, then I get combat fatigue and uninstall the game after realizing "hey this ♥♥♥♥ sucks".

217882080.review:
Great game with sophisticated production quality. Worthy successor to AOE II.

If you've played AOE II before, this is an improvement over essentially everything that may have made the previous instalment short of a truly great simulator, i.e. the absurdities (stone walls torn down by infantry, uncrossable partial constructions...) and the dullness (civilisations in AOE IV are truly unique, while in AOE II they differ by little in comparison).

The campaign is featured with BBC-grade documentaries (base game, not the the DLC one unfortunately) and fun facts that help immersion. Not all missions are compelling (again, looking at Sultans DLC), but otherwise it provides a really fun, quite immersive historical experience and entry point to the game's mechanics.

There is a thriving community and ranked games match you with people in your league, so you can be as competitive as you like. Playing against AI and people are completely different, and your wit makes or breaks even the most OP strategy.

The DLCs are a little pricey (at least drop the price for the Knights-of!) but there is nothing that stops the base civilisations from being enough diversity to sustain tens of hours of fun.

217845672.review: While I do find myself missing some aspects of AOE III, this is still a great addition to the series.

I love the unique perks of different organizations, like leveling up with the Abbasids and the villages with the Chinese. The graphics are much nicer than age of empires 3, but I did enjoy play age of empires. The story line was much more invested, and the ones in aoe 4 are more historical, which is cool too.

217827994.review: I'm going to review this game from Single Player perspective only - This is one of the worst games I've ever played and hands down the worst age of empires game ever made in my opinion, having played all of them. To start you have very poor control over your units, the game forces AI to do half the job, which usually results in your units attacking buildings mid-combat or running around in circles while getting killed. The campaign is extremely unimaginative and dull, and jumps around constantly in timeline and does not develop any personalities or characters in an even remotely memorable or entertaining way, the narrative reads like a shallow oversimplified Wikipedia article about medieval history then jumps around before you have the opportunity to invest at all in what just happened. The graphics are incredibly dull, bland and lifeless, the colors are muted and vague, and all of the icons are monochrome, further adding to the monotony and lifelessness of the UI and visual vibe. There are barely any real innovations to the actually gameplay mechanics aside from planting stakes in the ground for foot soldiers. Ambush and concealment ideas are borrowed from games like Ancestors Legacy or COH. In the campaign, city management feels like a meaningless distraction since the game expects you to micro squads of units and carefully manage them for the entire duration of the mission in tactics-style like in Ancestors Legacy, while micromanaging an inordinate number of tasks to build your city and keep troops coming - basically the game has no clear identity - it wants to be a real time tactics game like Ancestors Legacy and yet burdens you with the minutia of managing and building a town to the level of the typical AOE system - the balance results in irritating and unfocused gameplay. This game has no soul. I regret buying it and failing to return it within the return window. Absolutely do not recommend it. If you’re done with AOE 2 DE, Play AOE 3 DE instead – that one is very well done.

217415379.review:
Well this is a step up for the series. It feels very much parked in AoE2 territory, but with all the good things from AoE3 without the confusing bits, and modern graphical and sound improvements to go with it. What I liked the most was the historical detail and video cutscenes and narration which give the game some History Channel vibes without going too heavily into documentary territory. Might also teach you a few things about history by the time you're done.
I also like that they focus on the strategy of large battles (introducing the first campaign a bit of Total War strategy)

Things I didn't like included the camera (doesn't zoom out enough) and the scrolling which is very annoying. 
I hope they get more campaigns into the series and revisit other ancient empires including the important ones like Rome again to retell history in higher graphical fidelity. 
My only other concern is that the initial campaigns are few, any future campaigns are all going to be gated behind paid DLC and that pains me a bit.

217353262.review:
For context, I'm a 30-something year old who grew up with all kinds of obscure RTS games. I played some Starcraft 2 and AOE2 multiplayer, but I stopped playing that many years ago. I thought that I'm just getting too old for this hectic, adrenaline-driven gameplay and I turned my attention to 4x games.

AOE4 got me back into the 1v1 action, which I would never guessed would ever, ever happen again. The civ variety and strategic diversity is nuts among contemporary games of the genre. The sound design is amazing, the game is IMO pleasant to look at and the community is generally pretty nice. Though it could improve a bit when it comes to QOL features, it's way better than the monumental 2nd part of the series (which I also love).

While the devs do take their time with updates and patches, and while their communication could still be better, it improved massively in the last two years in which I have been playing and following the game. The balance is also pretty damn good, you can play any civ on ladder and almost any strategy (if you are in the lower or medium leagues, that is). Queue times are nothing to complain about.

I never looked much into the singleplayer content. The base campaigns are interesting, but kinda meh in terms of gameplay. Others are saying that the first DLC got much better campaigns. What I can say, is that the Crucible mode, dropped in the latest DLC, is a massive addition and a lot of fun. It looks like the devs will expand on singleplayer in the near future. Two DLCs are lined up for 2026 alone.

If you are a fan of classic RTS gameplay and especially of multiplayer, and if you wanna try a modern game and not the often regurgitated classics, then AOE4 is simply the best one out there. I caught fire for the ladder grind again - and for that I'm grateful to the game and the devs. See you on the frontlines.

Age of Empires IV is a fantastic evolution of the classic RTS formula. The gameplay mechanics feel well-balanced and rewarding, with enough strategic depth to keep each match engaging while still being accessible. The controls and overall operation are intuitive, making it easy to jump in whether you’re a long-time fan or new to the series.

Visually, the game looks great. The graphics are clean and polished, and the animations and battle effects make large-scale fights feel alive without overwhelming the screen. Each civilization has its own personality, which adds variety and keeps gameplay fresh.

Overall, AoE IV strikes a great balance between classic Age of Empires charm and modern RTS design. I’m really enjoying the experience and hope to see even more civilizations released in the future to expand the already solid foundation.

Highly recommended for RTS fans.

216495123.review:
Not Recommended

I played this game at launch and decided to come back after a long break.
Unfortunately, the game doesn’t launch at all anymore.

I’m fairly tech-savvy and spent ~5 hours diagnosing this. I went through official forums, Reddit, and deep system troubleshooting. Together with extensive testing and the loving buddy GPT, I ruled out:

Steam / launch methods
Antivirus & firewall
DirectX (legacy + modern)
GPU & drivers
Windows edition
Gaming Services
Visual C++ runtimes
Exploit protection settings

What’s left is a Windows 11 24H2 security virtualization issue (Memory Integrity) silently blocking the game. No error message, no crash log — just “Running” → nothing.

At this point, I’m honestly too bored of the “FIX your game yourself” playstyle. I didn’t sign up to debug kernel-level Windows security just to play an RTS that used to work.

The game itself might be good — if it launches.
Right now, the barrier to entry is absurd.

Fix your game.

216109928.review:
"Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition", developed by Relic Entertainment and published by Xbox Game Studios, is a direct and ambitious successor to the legendary real-time strategy franchise, modernizing the classic formula with stunning visuals, fresh mechanics, and a renewed focus on historical authenticity.

The game excels in delivering a visually spectacular and tactile RTS experience. Battles feel weighty and cinematic, the distinct civilizations offer meaningfully different playstyles and technologies, and the landmark system for age advancement adds a satisfying layer of map control. The single-player campaigns are a particular highlight, blending engaging documentary-style live-action video segments with traditional missions to create an exceptionally immersive and educational historical narrative. However, the initial launch suffered from a relative lack of content and strategic depth compared to its predecessor's decades of refinement. While updates have addressed much of this, some players may still find the overall civ count and meta-game variety less rich than in Age of Empires II: Definitive Edition.

Pros:

-Spectacular presentation with beautiful visuals, immersive sound design, and excellent documentary-style campaigns.
-Civilians and landmark systems create meaningful strategic variety and a satisfying sense of progression.
-Core RTS gameplay is polished, accessible, and delivers thrilling, large-scale battles.

Cons:

-Despite updates, the overall number of civilizations and strategic meta-diversity still feels more limited compared to AoE2.
-Some pathfinding and unit AI behaviors can be inconsistent, especially in dense formations.
-The initial content offering at launch felt lean, though the Anniversary Edition has largely rectified this.

Overall, "Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition" is a confident and highly polished modern evolution of the franchise that successfully captures the epic spirit of its predecessors. While it may not yet match the sheer depth and legacy content of AoE II: DE, its superb presentation—particularly the excellent short video documentaries woven into the campaign—and strong core gameplay make it a must-play for both returning fans and new strategy enthusiasts. 8.8/10

215836337.review:
I, a man sworn to sword and bow, do set down these words as witness to my long campaign within Age of Empires IV. For one hundred and sixty hours by the reckoning of the clock, I stood upon its fields of war. I marshaled hosts, raised keeps of stone and timber, and loosed volleys of arrows against the foes of many kingdoms. In this contest of wit and arms, I found both honor and great delight. The game rewarded patience, foresight, and the careful placing of men, much as true war does.

Yet hear now of the bitter turn of fate.

Upon a day most strange and ill-omened, when a new update descended as if by sorcery from the heavens, the game that had so long answered my call would no longer rise at all. Where once banners unfurled and drums sounded, there was only silence. The battlefield would not open. No host could be summoned.

I sought counsel among my fellows, other soldiers of this realm, and together we tried every remedy known to us. Files were checked, rites repeated, and settings altered, yet all our efforts were as arrows loosed into a gale. Nothing availed us.

At last I turned to the lords who forged this world, hoping for guidance. Their only answer was to point me toward a scroll of support, a page of common remedies which I had already scoured with all due diligence. There was no new wisdom there, no hand extended to lift a fallen soldier back into the fight.

Thus I stand, a veteran archer barred from the field by forces unseen, my long service rendered meaningless by a single ill-wrought decree. Age of Empires IV is a grand and noble game, worthy of praise for its depth and craft. But what value has a fine bow, if its string is cut without warning, and its maker offers only a pamphlet in reply?

Such is my testament.

215573833.review:
Overall State of the Game The game suffers from a severe lack of features and updates are extremely rare. When we finally get "new" content, the civilizations are often broken, introducing nonsensical mechanics that further destabilize the game. Critical balance changes and bug fixes arrive far too late, leaving the meta in a stale or broken state for months.

User Interface & Experience
Outdated UI: The interface is clunky, not user-friendly, and feels decades old.
Lack of Data: There are no proper in-game statistics or detailed data for players to analyze their performance.
Steam Overlay Bug: A frustrating bug makes the keyboard register multiple inputs simultaneously when chatting via the Steam overlay, making communication impossible.
Localization: Translation errors still show incorrect values, proving a lack of attention to detail.

Ranked & Matchmaking Issues
Map Pool Discrepancy: Using different map pools for Quick Play and Ranked is a terrible decision. It kills the motivation for 1v1 practice when you can't play on the maps that actually matter for your rank.
The "Gold Rank" Problem: Starting brand-new players in Gold rank is absurd. New players should start at the bottom and climb based on merit. Currently, matchmaking is a gamble where you often get teammates with zero game knowledge.
Rank Disparity: Due to a shrinking player base, Bronze and Gold players are frequently matched together, creating unfair and unfun matches.
Latency: The system often matches players with distant servers, forcing us to play with unplayable high latency.

Team Games & Griefing
Early Surrenders: In ranked team games, solo-queue teammates often surrender immediately at the start of the match for no reason.
No Penalty System: There is zero punishment for players who sabotage matches this way, forcing the rest of the team to suffer through a 3v4 or lose points.

Technical Flaws: The "Play Again" feature and general lobby management are poorly implemented. Even stopping a match search is bugged; the game can still find a match after you've cancelled the queue.

215561744.review:
If you're a fan of Age of Empires, you'll like this one. It's basiaclly Age of Empires 3 with much better graphics, maps and more possibilities. The game runs smoothly (you'd bloody hope so on a 4080 Super...).
Whether you're looking for a challenge or for some chill fortress building experience, you'll get what you want in this game as it is highly customizable. 
The factions are fun and diverse enough I suppose, provided you get the DLC's. I guess the devs are trying to make sure not to make one faction too OP by simply not creating too many units or economy differencies. 
Point is, it's all there and it's really up to you to make your game what you want it to be. 

However, the one thing that bothers me is the usual lack of initial content in "big" games and this is no exception. The first new content they added was paying, and that very early after release. The nice thing is that DLC's also include new campaigns. I said there was enough variety between factions, but only DLC factions offer a truly different experience.

Speaking of the campaign, though it can be a bit silly and frustrating, it's generally well done and actually manages to teach you a thing or two about the History of the factions you get to play (they keep it light). It's not a documentary, but they walk you through the headlines with adequate background, including footage of the actual battlfields and such.

Finally, I must say I only ever do PVE. I am a big RTS PVP player, but this is my go to when I'm just looking for a chill, rich and highly satisfying experience. 

In other words, it is a fun well made game and it's current price is acceptable. The DLC's aren't cheap, but that's just what it is... I got them...

215201347.review: Amazing game! I have very few complaints and far fewer than the average game. The devs poured their heart and soul into this game! The best way to describe this game is AoE:II Remake with higher attention to detail. I wish that AoE had followed the history timeline and had been set in maybe 1840s-1940s but I'm not complaining. This game did the old AoE games justice. It feels like they went back to basics for the foundation of the game and added just a sprinkle of what was in AoE:III and just a smidge of innovation and clearly a whole lot of love and passion for Age of Empires. I love this game and I don't even play AoE:I or II but I do still play III sometimes because it's just a unique game that stands on it's own. I actually wish AoE IV was more like III. One thing that really annoys me is treasures, out posts and heroes are gone. All these things could have easily worked in this game. The game being a little too bare is my biggest complaint and really it's not a bad thing if you didn't care for those features or never played AoE: III. Final takeaway is this game is truly great and you can't go wrong but I also recommend playing the first 3 if you haven't maybe before playing this game. I started with AoE III so it doesn't matter what order but having a feel for where the series came from could enrich the experience, They are all some of the best games around. The number one complaint across all of them is the 200 unit pop system. This game needs an unlimited pop like Stronghold and C&C. We have the technology and mods have proven it works...

214167091.review:
The game seems pretty fun, but it's extremely unstable. Three times I tried to play the tutorial mission, each time the game hard crashed within 5-10 minutes. One time it crashed just from lowering the shadow quality. The only way I got through on the fourth time was to set all graphical settings to the lowest possible, and even then I'm not sure if I just got lucky.

Checking the Steam forums for the game, I counted six threads on the first page alone complaining about crashes. Some seem to suspect it has to do with some kind of networking fault, others blame graphical driver or hardware issues. Either way, that's a really bad ratio, and an awful first impression for any new player.

My crash logs showed a network timeout, about 100 milliseconds before another log claimed that the game "submitted an invalid instruction to the GPU", so I dunno. Maybe it's one, maybe it's both. I updated drivers and it made no difference, and I didn't pay twenty euros to play Tech Support Simulator, so I'm giving up. Given the amount of crash reports from so many players, I'm sure they've got plenty of data to find the cause by now.

For now though, I can't recommend spending money on a game that doesn't function. It looks fun though, and seems actively supported, so I'm resisting the urge to refund and just hoping for a couple of good stability patches.

214155310.review:
This is actually a good game with good gameplay, and if you get it on a sale (as I did) I would say it is worth buying. But I will still go with a "no" on the recommendation. So lets look at the things. 

THE POSITIVE
+ Good gameplay (fun to play)
+ Good graphics 

THE NEGATIVE
- The menues are hard to navigate 
- Only small maps available (a little larger one is available after testing a map, but not from the start) 
- Basically no strategy needed on the field, strongest army win, period!
- Still not possible to set an initial time of "no-attacks" at the beginning of the game. 
- Most randomly generated map-types are DLC and need to be payed for (this is really bad) 
- No historical maps available (atleast I havent found them) 
- No preset, well crafted gamemaps available in multiplayer/Skirmish (only random generated)
- The map editor is extremely unintuitive and a real danger to your bloodpressure. It is not for the casual map-maker. You need to spend hours to get into it. Must be a sadist that created this editor. 
- Many fractions are DLC 
- Some bugs still remain even though the community has reported them 
- Still no auto-explore on the shouts (you are stuck with the micro management from earlier version)
- Still no auto-repair on the workers 
- The Gigantic map option is hidden inside the megarandom generator (after I used this generator the gigantic map was available in all maps, and it is not really that gigantic). 

It is not a bad game, and it is fun to play. But the fact that you need to pay for basic stuff like fractions and auto-generated maps feels like a money-grab. And the lack of tactics reqired in the field makes it still feel like a build-simulator rather than a strategy game.

213992129.review:
I really enjoy Age of Empires IV as an RTS, but the historical criteria used to include or exclude civilizations are deeply inconsistent.

Spain is often dismissed with the argument that it “did not exist as a unified state in the Middle Ages”. However, this logic does not hold when compared with other civilizations already in the game:

France also did not exist as a modern nation-state for most of the medieval period, yet it is included based on dynastic and institutional continuity.

The Holy Roman Empire was never a unified state in practice, yet it is represented as a single civilization.

Meanwhile, the Iberian Peninsula is completely ignored despite:

The Visigothic Kingdom having ruled almost the entire peninsula with common laws and a central capital centuries before medieval France was consolidated.

The clear political and military unity of Castile and Aragon by the late 15th century.

Figures like Gonzalo Fernández de Córdoba (“El Gran Capitán”), whose military reforms directly shaped early modern European warfare and fit perfectly within AoE IV’s late-medieval timeframe.

The absence of any Iberian civilization (Castile, Aragon, Portugal, Spain, Al-Andalus, or Visigoths) is not a historical necessity but a design choice — and an inconsistent one.

If AoE IV is willing to simplify history for gameplay reasons (which is understandable), then Iberian civilizations deserve the same consideration as France or the HRE.

213498953.review:
Age of Empires IV really shines in how different each civilization feels. Every civ (and sometimes its variants) has its own theme, mechanics, visuals, and playstyle. This can be a bit overwhelming to learn at first, but thematically I think it’s the best Age of Empires game ever.

The strategic depth is very different from AoE II. For example, you can’t fully block your base with buildings anymore, since there’s a one-tile gap between them. This forces you to invest in walls and spend more resources on defense only, which changes how you approach the feudal and castle age. There's also a lot of economic buildings and upgrades on specific civs that will make you want to take more control of the resources on the map, or make you better with farms so you can play defensively. And lastly there's the Sacred Sites scattered on the map which you can capture for 10 mins to win the game instead of having to destroy the base of a very defensive player.

Combat is also a bit slower, and siege units don’t deal friendly fire. So you don’t need to be an APM god to control fights optimally, making battles feel more manageable and tactical.

213467520.review:
Has a ton of unique ways to play, battles have good replayability to them due to all the random aspects, civilization uniqueness and there are tons of gameplay mechanics. Game seems pretty balanced at least to a certain extent (not that I will get to experience imbalance at my currently play level).

The game will take a while to master or get better at reducing all of your bottlenecks, juggling different actions at once, keybinds, quick actions, having as much automation as you can, knowing how early you need to set up a defense versus progressing your civilization, learning game mechanics such as unit counters, trading with trader units, relics and religious gameplay. research, etc.

This game has a nice feature for those that don't like getting experience while failing miserably over and over as you learn. You can observe others who have the observe setting enabled in the random matchmaker and take from their winning gameplay what you what you think is valuable or efficient.

Making micro adjustments to your gameplay sequences, click order, method of completing tasks to get marginal advantages over how you used to accomplish a same task is pretty satisfying.

212621180.review:
I had more fun with this game compared to StarCraft 2, because there is less emphasis on fast fingers and subconscious decision-making. You can chill more and actually enjoy looking at your forces crushing enemies, whereas in SC2 you would have to micro everything all the time and check on your bases in the meantime. You can build large production cohorts that supply new forces to a big prolonged battle (I know this doesn't appeal to tacticians, but I like mindless meat grinder fights).

Sound design is outstanding, even outside of the RTS genre - fights sound great and realistic, SFXs are of high quality and I've never heard such dynamic systems that make the thunder of horses so satisfying to listen to. It makes big battles even more grandiose (especially with cannons shooting walls or castles, this is unmatched!).

There are a few things that stop this game from being the best RTS of all time, like the lack of resuming games from replays, rejoining a match after being disconnected and too much delay between commands and unit actions, but I still recommend it!

212553119.review:
Age of Empires IV shines brightest through its campaigns, which serve as the most engaging part of the experience. Presented in a documentary-style format, each campaign blends narration, historical footage, and well-structured missions to bring medieval conflicts to life. These stories feel educational without losing the excitement of commanding armies and shaping key moments in history.

Gameplay within the campaigns is accessible and satisfying, offering classic base-building and large-scale battles with enough variation to keep missions interesting. The mix of defensive scenarios, sieges, and objective-focused battles adds welcome variety.  For fans of history-driven strategy, Age of Empires IV’s campaigns are its greatest strength.

Although still overshadowed by Age of Empires II (AoE2), AoE4 tries to blend the best of its two predecessors (AoE2, AoE3 and AoM) by balancing between having unique civilizations and common units/tactics. This makes for good civilization replayability, especially if you pruchase the expansions.

The expansions demonstrate the developer's desire to keep building on this base with new civilizations and campaigns, with the latest ones favouring civilization over campaign content. Some players may dislike this, although the variant civilizations increase replayability.

All-in-all a fun and worthwhile experience, if still eclipsed by AoE2 (as all Age of Empires games are to certain extent).

211809809.review:
One of the best RTS ever designed. It is slower and more complex than Starcraft. You will have to manage 4 major resources instead of 2. You will progress through 4 tech tiers instead of 3. There are 22 factions instead of 3, with some similarities between them.
My favorite part is the procedural map generation. The mapping system for this game is incredibly varied and dynamic. Each map style provides a very different type of metagame.
Good defensive options for turtling up if you like that style. Siege is an important strategic layer in Age of Empires.
Starcraft gives a faster tighter game while Age of Empires gives a slower more complex game. All RTS fans should experience both.

Age of Empires IV made me feel like a tactical genius… until the AI reminded me I’m not.

Age of Empires IV is everything I wanted: gorgeous graphics, satisfying gameplay, and the sweet illusion that I know what I’m doing. The units look amazing, the battles feel epic, and the moment I discovered I can zoom in to watch tiny soldiers panic in HD… chef’s kiss.

I was having the time of my life building my empire — until a bunch of angry horsemen reminded me that walls are not just decorative. Still, every loss feels fun, every win feels legendary, and every trebuchet feels like a warm hug from the medieval gods.

Highly recommended if you enjoy strategy, history, or the sound of villagers saying “Yes M'lord” while you ruin their lives.

210756973.review: AoE IV stays true to the core essence of the series—gather resources, build your economy, train armies, and outmaneuver your opponents. What makes it stand out is how each civilization feels uniquely handcrafted. It blends historical authenticity, tactical depth, and high production quality into a polished, endlessly replayable package.

210738632.review:
Great RTS, probably the most polished release since Starcraft 2.

It's essentially Age of Empires 2 but better. Civs are no longer carbon copies of each other and have unique elements to their playstyles (unlike Age 2 where every Civ was more or less the same with some units replaced with a unique unit). Generic unit models and buildings are also unique for civs which adds so much to the flavor of the game. For example, the generic archer has a unique model across the Mongols, Chinese, French, Abassid, Delhi, etc. You also no longer have the problem of, say, Chinese or Japanese sharing an "Asian" structure tileset (always bothered me how the Koreans in Age 2 had very Chinese looking buildings).

Although sometimes it is difficult to tell units apart in an army composition. For the most part units tend to look pretty similar to one another so I'm having to rely on the group selection icons at the bottom to isolate unit types.

210584653.review:
UPDATE:
I promised I will update this after the beIow mentioned issue is fixed. This was done with the last patch.

Great Game! Buy it and you will enjoy it.

Here is the original review:

"I will delete/update this review after the following is "fixed":

In my over 600 hours in this game I mostly played nomad ffa. First in custom lobbies and later on as quick matches once they added this mode officially.

I really like the chaos and unforgiving nature of megarandom maps. No two matches are the same. Sometimes you have a godlike spawn and roll over the lobby, sometimes you have to make the best out of some wasteland around you and get rolled over. Or you improvise good and convert a bad start into a great victory. Sometimes you spawn all alone and sometimes you find yourself in between five other players. It was and still is highly addicting. The opposite of SBMM if you will. Sure some megarandom map spawns are utter garbage, but that is very rare and the need to make the best out of the situation a new match throws you in made me a better player overall.


Since the last big update I mostly quit playing all together as they added two new maps to the nomad map pool that are pre made. It takes away most of the randomness. I know where ressources are, I know where enemies will probably build and also I know which civs will perform good or bad on those maps. Gone are the days of chaos, improvisation and triumphant victory.

As this mode is my overall aoe4 experience and they took it away I personally can't recommend this game right now if you are any like me."

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Fantastic RTS Experience – Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition

Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition is a perfect blend of classic RTS charm and modern improvements. As someone who has played the Age of Empires series for years, this edition truly feels like the definitive way to experience AoE4.

The graphics and animations are beautiful, with a clean visual style that makes battles easy to read yet still epic in scale. The civilization diversity has improved tremendously—each faction feels unique, especially with the Anniversary content that adds more depth and replayability.

The campaigns are cinematic and educational, presenting history in a documentary-style format that is both engaging and refreshing. Multiplayer is smooth, well-balanced, and constantly evolving with updates. The added quality-of-life improvements, extra maps, and game modes in the Anniversary Edition make it feel like a complete package.

Whether you’re a long-time AoE fan or new to RTS games, AOE4: Anniversary Edition is absolutely worth playing. It captures the heart of the franchise while delivering a polished, modern real-time strategy experience.

209878353.review: As a former StarCraft 2 player, this game is so much fun. Much more relaxing to play casually and exciting to play competitively, but from all the things that stand out the most to me for what makes this game so great is its immersion, which is not something you expect out of an RTS. Not only are the graphics nice, but I absolutely love all of the decorative elements that generate (ie. small yards next to houses) that make the landscape constantly dynamic and interesting. From an immersion perspective, however, the decorative details pale in comparison to the expertly crafted audio experience. This game truly defines its own category of a S+ tier audio design. The background music for each civ, the gibberish units speak with accents based on civ(never gets old), the battle crys, the elephants and cannons, and the little cowbell sound when your scout finds a sheep. This game puts you in a different world and keeps you there.

209346540.review:
I like this game, but there are several serious issues that prevent me from recommending it.

1. Matchmaking is extremely buggy. About 2 out of every 5 matchmaking attempts crash with various errors. This has been a widely discussed problem for years, yet it feels like the developers haven’t addressed it properly.
2. Matchmaking balance is very poor. Players of vastly different skill levels are often placed in the same match. In 3v3 quick games, I’ve frequently seen teams with Platinum and Diamond players matched against Bronze or Silver players. Ranked mode is slightly better, but in casual matches it seems like balance isn’t a priority at all.
3. The user interface is very slow. Menus and screens take a long time to load, and match results can take 5–10 minutes to appear in your profile after a game ends. This lack of optimization makes the overall experience feel sluggish and unpolished.
4. In the latest update, they added "magenta" and "dark green" colors. This is the most ridiculous "improvement" of all. When your teammate is pink and the enemy is magenta—and they clash with one another—it is absolutely impossible to tell them apart when they fight with each other; everything just blends into one solid mess. The same goes for green and dark green.

I am deeply disappointed with the current state of the game. I would gladly change my review to a recommendation if the developers fixed these issues, as I genuinely want to love and support this game.

209325908.review:
Old enough to call itself, "Anniversary Edition," and still feeling like an unpolished 1.0 release.

The quality of life features are what doom AoE4.  Or rather, their lack thereof.  Building a skirmish requires you to tab through multiple pages and hand configure everything, every game, because it resets everything to defaults instead of saving your previous settings.  A bunch of common functions, include Select Next Idle Villager, and Build Farm, do not have hotkeys and hotkeys for them cannot be set.  A lot behaviors that have been in the series since at least Age of Empire 2, back in 1999, are absent here, like Villagers understanding that only one of them can work a farm at time.

If this were an AoE clone, that might fly.  But this is a main line Age of Empires game.  The 2007 release of Age of Empires 3, as well as AoE2 Definitive Edition and Age of Mythology Retold all have features that this game is missing.

No single bad QoL is backbreaking, but they all pile up.  Converting your villagers from hunting to farming is something that was no big deal in any game except this one, where it's an APM nightmare.  Only being able to select all of your idle villagers at once is another thing that makes gameplay so much more difficult than it needs to be.

And then the aggressive Live Service monetization butts in, making it loud and clear what civs and game modes that your peasant ass doesn't have access to, just for that little extra upthrust middle finger.

Bottom line is that there are four Age of Empires games currently being maintained on Steam, and somehow, the one with 14 extra years of game design experience to inform is that worst by a clear margin.  Age of Empires 4 isn't a bad game, but it definitely isn't a good one.

In terms of single player, the campaigns gameplay are kinda meh, but the historical accuracy for each campaign scenario is gosh dang amazing. Fortunately (or unfortunately) the best single player mode in AOE IV are locked in DLC, it's the Crucible that comes with Dynasties of The East DLC. It's AOE IV roguelite take and I think it's the first proper roguelite RTS I encounter (Given I usually play roguelite tower def and god know how many ♥♥♥♥♥♥ money grab roguelite out there).

I wish there was a maybe option. The mechanics are good and decent replayability (though the AI is horrible), but to me the civs are pretty bland, and instead of adding new civs, they usually add a variant of a current one. Basically reskinning it and adding a few mechanics + a price tag for it. On top of a few bland civs, there is no customization except extremely simple things like your player banner. What really doesn't make sense to me, is the many daily and event quests and such for xp and leveling, yet there's about 0.01% incentive to level up (like player banners and a statue in your city for rewards). I wish it had progression/customization similar to some previous titles. (personally i think the level of customization for your armies that AOE online had would work well in a live service such as this and another example of this would be Starcraft 2).

Content is about 1/10th of previous games like AOE 2: here's the store description for AOE 2: "42 Civilizations, 229 Campaign Missions, Singleplayer, Co-Op, Multiplayer, In-Game Editor, Mods, Cross-play...It is good to be the king"

This game currently has about 12 civs (no i do not count variants) and roughly 75 campaign missions. This is if you buy all DLCs, which aren't cheap. Compare that to previous titles and its not even close. By time they catch up on content (if ever) it will cost you $200+

I truly believe it gets so many positive reviews bc of the state the genre is in currently, but we shouldn't just lower standards.

### Один за сбой сервера Steam, один за сбой игрока Age of Empires IV. ###

【【【Если вы недовольны, можете прекратить выпуск DLC. Это первый случай, когда крупная корпорация допустила такую ошибку. Hollow Knight: Silksong столкнулась с меньшим количеством проблем, но эта компания работает в меньших масштабах, чем ваша, и предлагает более низкие цены.】】】

Игра не запускается после обновлений. (Это приводит к прекращению работы из-за невозможности загрузить или сгенерировать данные конфигурации игры. Проблема связана с отсутствием прав доступа к каталогу или аномальными правами доступа к файлам/папкам.)

### Einmal für den Absturz des Steam-Servers, einmal für den Absturz des Spielers von Age of Empires IV. ###

【【【Sollten Sie unzufrieden sein, können Sie die Veröffentlichung von DLC einstellen. Dies ist das erste Mal, dass ein großes Unternehmen einen solchen Fehler begangen hat. Hollow Knight: Silksong hatte weniger Probleme, aber dieses Unternehmen ist kleiner als Ihres und hat niedrigere Preise.】】】

Das Spiel kann nach Updates nicht gestartet werden. (Dies führt zur Beendigung, da die Spielkonfigurationsdaten nicht geladen oder generiert werden können. Das Problem liegt an fehlenden Verzeichniszugriffsrechten oder anomalen Datei-/Ordnerberechtigungen.)

### Uno per il crash del server Steam, uno per il crash del giocatore di Age of Empires IV. ###

【【【Se non sei soddisfatto, puoi interrompere il rilascio dei DLC. È la prima volta che una grande azienda commette un errore del genere. Hollow Knight: Silksong ha riscontrato meno problemi, ma quell'azienda opera su scala minore rispetto alla tua e ha un prezzo più basso.】】】

Il gioco non si avvia dopo gli aggiornamenti. (Ciò comporta la chiusura del gioco a causa dell'impossibilità di caricare o generare i dati di configurazione. Il problema deriva dalla mancanza di autorizzazioni di accesso alla directory o da autorizzazioni anomale per file/cartelle).

### 스팀 서버 붕괴에 대한 한 건, 《에이지 오브 제국 IV》 플레이어 붕괴에 대한 한 건.###

【【【불만이 있다면 DLC 출시를 중단할 수 있습니다. 대기업이 이런 실수를 저지른 건 이번이 처음입니다. 『에인션트 나이트: 실의 노래』는 문제가 거의 없었고, 해당 회사는 귀사보다 규모가 작으며 가격도 더 저렴했습니다.】】】

업데이트 완료 후 게임을 실행할 수 없습니다. (게임 설정 데이터를 로드하거나 생성할 수 없어 종료됩니다. 이 문제는 디렉토리 접근 권한 부족 또는 파일/폴더 권한 이상으로 인해 발생합니다.)

### Steamサーバーのクラッシュに対する声明、そして『エイジ・オブ・エンパイア IV』プレイヤーのクラッシュに対する声明。###

【【【不満ならDLCの配信を停止すればよい。大企業がこのようなミスを犯すのは初めてだ。『空心騎士：糸の歌』の問題は少なく、同社は御社より規模が小さく、価格も安い。】】】

更新後、ゲームを起動できなくなった。（ゲーム設定データの読み込みまたは作成に失敗したため終了。この問題はディレクトリへのアクセス権限不足、またはファイル/フォルダの権限異常が原因である。）

### واحد بسبب تعطل خادم Steam، وواحد بسبب تعطل لاعب Age of Empires IV. ###

【【【إذا كنت غير راضٍ، يمكنك التوقف عن إصدار DLC. هذه هي المرة الأولى التي ترتكب فيها شركة كبرى مثل هذا الخطأ. واجهت Hollow Knight: Silksong مشاكل أقل، لكن تلك الشركة تعمل على نطاق أصغر من شركتك وتقدم أسعارًا أقل.】】】

فشل تشغيل اللعبة بعد التحديثات. (ينتج عن ذلك إنهاء اللعبة بسبب عدم القدرة على تحميل أو إنشاء بيانات تكوين اللعبة. تنبع المشكلة من فقدان أذونات الوصول إلى الدليل أو أذونات الملفات/المجلدات غير الطبيعية.)

207695140.review:
The **intro of *Age of Empires IV*** sets an epic and educational tone, blending cinematic storytelling with historical immersion.

Here’s a breakdown of it:

🎬 **Visuals:**

* The intro opens with **stunning aerial footage** of a misty battlefield, seamlessly transitioning between **modern drone shots** of historical sites and **CGI recreations** of ancient battles.
* You see **armies clashing**, siege engines rolling, and empires rising and falling — symbolizing the passage of ages and human progress.

🎙️ **Narration:**

* A calm, **British-accented female narrator** (the same voice used throughout the campaign) sets the theme:

  > “History is not just the story of the past — it’s the story of who we are...”
* The voiceover explains how from humble beginnings, humanity built civilizations, fought wars, and shaped the world through innovation and courage.

🎶 **Music:**

* The soundtrack swells with **orchestral and choral tones**, echoing classic *Age of Empires* themes but more cinematic and dramatic — capturing both grandeur and nostalgia.

🏰 **Theme:**

* The intro’s purpose is to show that *Age of Empires IV* isn’t just a game about battles — it’s a **journey through the evolution of civilization**, teaching history while celebrating human achievement.

Would you like me to describe the **exact narration script** or the **symbolism of the visuals** (like the transitions between eras and empires)?

With Farming you didn't have too prepay a million copies of "seeds" too reseed farms. Among many others individual unit abilities.

207681994.review:
Aye, well now, lend yer ear, traveler, and I’ll tell ye plain and honest: there’s no finer work under the King’s sun than choppin’ wood. Nay, not plowin’ the fields, nor tendin’ the sheep, nor even swingin’ a sword for the lord’s banners. For wood—good strong wood—is the heart o’ the kingdom, and I, humble though I be, am the man what feeds that heart day by day with the swing of me axe.

I rise afore dawn, when the sky’s still black and the crows ain’t yet stirred. The ground be slick with dew, and the chill nips the nose right fierce. But I take it all in stride, for the forest calls to me like a friend waitin’ by the fire. The axe rests heavy on me shoulder, her handle smooth from years of grip. She’s got a good edge—sharp as the Queen’s wit, mayhap—and when she bites, she bites deep.

Aye, when the first swing lands, and that crack echoes through the glade—by God, it’s like music! The tree shivers, moans, and then leans gentle, like a noble bowin’ to the King, before she falls, slow and proud. I step back respectful-like, hat in me hand, and mutter a quiet, “Thank ye, m’lady oak.” For every tree’s got a spirit, sure as men’ve got souls. Cut one without thanks, and she’ll curse yer axe to dullness or drop a limb right on yer fool head. Seen it happen, I have.

But I’ve always shown me manners proper, and the forest treats me fair. The air smells o’ pine and earth and the sweet sap that drips like honey from the wound. Me boots sink in the loam, soft as bread fresh from the oven. Birds chatter high above, as if gossipin’ on which fool will swing the hardest today. I wipes the sweat from me brow, plant me feet wide, and keep at it.

Some lads tire o’ the work. “Tom,” they say, “don’t it wear on ye, swingin’ all day like a beast o’ burden?” But I tell ’em, “Aye, it wears the arms, but it strengthens the soul.” For every swing’s a prayer, every crack of timber a hymn to honest labor. There’s peace in the rhythm, ye see—swing, thunk, swing, crack, drag the log, stack it high. The world could burn or the King could die, but still the trees would stand, and I’d be there with me axe, keepin’ order in the chaos.

Come midday, the sun’s high and cruel, and the sweat runs down me back in rivers. I sit beneath the shade with a crust of bread, maybe an onion if I’m lucky, and listen to the wind whistle through the branches. Sometimes I fancy it speaks words I can near understand. “Well done,” it says, or “Easy now, lad.” Then I take up me axe again, and she hums with life, eager to work.

When I haul the logs back to the town, the folk nod in thanks. The builders shout, “Wood! We need more wood!” and I grin wide as the river. For there’s no house, no market, no blacksmith’s forge without what I’ve hewn. The town center itself, proud as any castle, stands ‘cause I fed her her bones. Ships that sail for gold and glory—they’re mine too, in a way. Siege towers, longbows, palisades, all o’ them birthed from the sweat of men like me. So when them knights go ridin’ into battle, shinin’ in steel, they ought raise a mug to us woodcutters, for their glory’s built on our timber.

Aye, and I’ll tell ye plain, I love me work more than they love their wars. What’s glory but wind? What’s fame but a whisper? But wood—wood stands. The tree ye fell today will warm the babes in winter, mend the walls come siege-time, build the bridge for trade. I reckon no knight nor priest could claim half so much good done by their hand.

I remember me da, Gods rest him, took me into the woods when I were but a lad. “Mark the tree’s lean,” he said, “and notch her true. The forest listens, mind ye that.” I thought him daft, but I learned quick enough. One wrong cut, one careless swing, and ye’ll find yourself under a trunk thicker than a church pillar. But respect her, and the forest’ll feed ye for life. And so she has. I’ve swung this same axe near twenty winters now, and though me back aches fierce come night, I’d not trade it for a throne.

The seasons change, aye, and each one’s got its charm. In spring, the woods wake gentle, sap runnin’ sweet and the smell o’ new life everywhere. Summer brings hard work and harder thirst, but also the song of crickets and the hum o’ bees. Autumn’s the best o’ all—leaves red as fire, the air crisp, the world seemin’ ready for rest. And winter… aye, winter’s cruel, but the woodpile I’ve built stands tall, and when I see the smoke risin’ from every chimney, I smile, knowin’ I’ve earned each wisp of it.

Sometimes, when I’m alone in the deeper parts of the forest, where no man’s trod in years, I fancy I see shapes in the fog—faces in the bark, eyes in the moss. The old gods, maybe, or the spirits o’ the trees. They watch me work, quiet-like, and I give a nod to show I mean no harm. The forest’s got a long memory. A fool who takes without care might not come back at all. But I’ve made me peace with her, I have. We understand one another.

Evenin’ comes, and I trudge back to the village, arms sore, shirt damp with sweat. The air smells o’ stew and smoke, and the sky’s burnin’ gold behind the thatch roofs. I drop me load by the stockpile, nod to the foreman, and take me pay—coin or bread or a mug o’ ale, don’t matter which. I sit by the fire, me hands blistered, me bones hummin’, and I feel rich as a lord. The tavern hums with talk—farmers, soldiers, fishers, all yammerin’ on. But when someone says, “Aye, thank the gods for wood, else we’d freeze come night,” I raise me cup high, grinnin’ wide through the soot.

Now and again, the town bell rings—“We’re under attack!”—and I see lads droppin’ their tools to take up arms. But me? I keep swingin’. For no matter who wins or loses, someone’ll need wood for rebuildin’. Even the ashes of war cry out for timber.

When I lie in me straw bed, the ache in me arms feels good—honest, earned. I think of the trees yet standin’, tall and proud in the moonlight, waitin’ for the dawn when I’ll come again. I think of the town growin’, walls risin’, people livin’ better ‘cause o’ the work I’ve done. And I smile in the dark.

So aye, I love me job. Love it more than coin, more than ale, more than sleep. For in every swing o’ the axe, I feel a piece o’ me soul ring out—strong, certain, eternal. When I’m gone, the trees I’ve felled will still stand tall in the beams of houses, the masts of ships, the frames of catapults. The world’ll keep on turnin’, and somewhere, some lad will pick up an axe and start anew.

And maybe he’ll stand in the dawn’s first light, hear the forest breathe, and smile as I did. And maybe he’ll say, quiet-like, “Aye… I love me work.”

When I was a kid, my elder brother used to play Age of Empires: Definitive Edition. I still remember asking him what game it was, and he said, “It’s an RPG and a really realistic one.” I asked if it was fun, and he smiled and said, “Yeah, but you’ve got to understand how to play it.” So I watched him for hours, trying to learn the basics. After a few days, I realized how amazing the game actually was — but then, unfortunately, my PC parts got stolen by a neighbor, and I had to quit gaming for a long time.

Five years later, I finally got a new PC — ironically, it was a gift from that same neighbor. I was so excited to install Age of Empires again… until I realized my new PC didn’t even have a disk drive! 😆 Still, no matter what, Age of Empires will always be my favorite game.

206770508.review:
Age of Empires IV delivers a confident modern RTS that respects the series’ DNA while pushing competitive pacing forward. Landmark-based aging, Sacred Site timers, and meaningful trade routes create constant, map-driven objectives that force interaction rather than passive booming. Civilizations feel genuinely asymmetrical—economy, tech access, and battlefield tools vary widely—yet remain readable after a short acclimation. Strong onboarding (campaigns, challenges, “Art of War”-style drills), a capable content editor, and steady live balance keep the game approachable for newcomers and strategically rich for ranked veterans. Visual clarity is solid for large battles, and quality-of-life touches—spectator tools, hotkey flexibility, and mod support—round out a polished package.

It isn’t flawless: big team fights can stress performance on lower-end rigs, siege vs. counter-siege balance periodically swings with patches, and the growing roster means matchup knowledge is a real barrier to mastery. Still, measured against today’s RTS field, AoE4 stands out for how often it produces decisive, skill-expressive timings and multi-lane map play. It’s a strategic heavyweight with modern conveniences—a professional, competitive RTS that remains welcoming, and one that has only improved with post-launch updates.
