206682830.review:
[h1][b][i]About This Game[/i][/b][/h1]

Celebrating its first year of delighting millions of global players, the award-winning and best-selling strategy franchise continues with [b]Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition[/b], putting you at the center of even more epic historical battles that shaped the world.

Featuring both familiar and innovative new ways to expand your empire in vast landscapes with stunning 4K visual fidelity, [b]Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition[/b] brings an evolved real-time strategy game to the next level in this celebratory new version that includes a host of free new content such as brand-new civilizations, new maps, additional in-game updates and languages, and new masteries, challenges, taunts and cheats – all at an amazing value that packs in more history than ever before!

Two New Civilizations, 8 New Maps - Lead the mighty Malians of West Africa as one of the richest trading nations of all-time as you focus on strong economic prowess in mining and investing in gold.

Or assemble one of the longest lasting empires in history with the Ottoman civilization and its well-trained military force, which features the largest gunpowder siege weapons available – the Great Bombards – mighty cannons that can fell any opponents who stand in your way.

Customize Your Game with Mods (Beta) - Chart your own course with powerful creator tools in the latest beta release of Mod Editor. Sculpt your own skirmish and multiplayer maps, craft unique mission scenarios, forge data driven tuning packs and envision new modes of play for Age of Empires IV.

Return to History – The past is prologue as you are immersed in a rich historical setting of 10 diverse civilizations across the world from the English to the Chinese to the Delhi Sultanate in your quest for victory. Build cities, manage resources, and lead your troops to battle on land and at sea in 4 distinct campaigns with 35 missions that span across 500 years of history from the Dark Ages up to the Renaissance.

An Age for All Players – Age of Empires IV is an inviting experience for new players with a tutorial system that teaches the essence of real-time strategy and a Campaign Story Mode designed for first time players to help achieve easy setup and success, yet is challenging enough for veteran players with new game mechanics, evolved strategies, and combat techniques.

Challenge the World – Jump online to compete, cooperate or spectate with up to 7 of your friends in PVP and PVE multiplayer modes that include ranked seasons and much more!

Choose Your Path to Greatness with Historical Figures – Live the adventures of Joan of Arc in her quest to defeat the English, or command mighty Mongol troops as Genghis Khan in his conquest across Asia. The choice is yours – and every decision you make will determine the outcome of history.

206419378.review:
I think AoE4 is a very good game, but I do have my issues with it. I'm primarily a multiplayer guy when it comes to aoe.

The game is beautiful, with outstanding sound design and really creative factions / playstyles. Openers feel simplified so that you're really accelerated into combat just a few minutes into the match. When the big battles hit it just feels epic. Easy recommend to anyone interested in RTS games.

But...holy hell....the ranked experience is a bit grueling. Some factions or units just do SO MUCH that it feels like there isn't a good baseline for what is really balanced. There's about 3 or 4 dozen things to know about each faction and that can get out of hand very quickly in a 2v2+.

A certain high mobility, high health, high damage, regenerating unit that can hit you in mass just a few minutes into the match really can warp the gameplay negatively around itself. Other factions have very cheesy/frustrating strats that are just not fun to deal with. As much as I appreciate the variety and vibrant vibe of AoE4, it just falls short to me against AoE2, which I consider to be a FAR superior game. 

In instances where you're very casual against AI or just played too much AoE2 already, then this game can be a lot of fun.

7/10

206321398.review:
Since I had only played AoE III before this, it took some time to get used to the way that AoE IV plays. I've seen people compare it to AoE II, but since I haven't played it I can't say if it's better or worse.

+ Nonetheless AoE IV is good, graphics are nice (as long as you don't zoom in too much and look too closely), the story/campaign in nice (the narrator makes it feel like a history lesson).
Gotta say I'm not a fan of the naval warfare, for me it just feels weird, might be due to lack of cannons since it takes place so early in history. However I really like the siege machines (something so satisfying about trebuchets yeeting big ass stones at the enemy). The thicker walls that let troops stand on them make your city look so impenetrable, combined with different outpost towers you can really build something visually stunning. Can't forget that buildings create paths, brick roads, bushes, gardens and other objects around them which just creates another level of immersion.
Sometimes I like play against easier level AIs just so i could just build the most visually stunning city.

- I would say the main problem of the game is the lack of civs, since most of the options are just variations of the same civ, even tho they play differently it just doesn't feel the same as the huge list of nations you get in AoE III.
There is one extra negative which doesn't affect the gameplay. The fact that you can't rotate buildings like in AoE III means you are restricted in terms of bulding visually immersive cities.

Personally I like AoE III a little bit more. There are alot more civs, alot of unique troops and some gameplay mechanics feel a bit better, but mostly it's just the nostalgia so I'm kinda biased.

206243609.review:
I've spent longer thinking about AOE IV than playing AOE IV at this point, mostly in trying to deduce why it is that I'm just not enjoying it despite my heavy involvement in RTS games since they got started. Not only that, but as one of many thousands of players who hold AOE II as their favourite game of all time, I was long looking forward to the next generation after AOE III. And while this is technically is that game, there's more to delivering a sequel than simply incrementing the title's numeral.

In reality, it's now clear that although I was looking for a sequel to AOE III, I was evidently specifically looking for a sequel to AOE III by Ensemble Studios. The more I play IV, the more painfully the reminder that Ensemble is no longer here and that Microsoft, after cannibalising them, continues to try to wear their skin as a hat and capitalise on their good reputation.

There are several things that IV does right, especially in terms of concepts that old Age of Empires manuals hinted as being mechanical goals in future games, such as being able to station archers onto walls and move flawlessly on and off of them through access points which also serve as tower segments. Some innovations from older games have also been carried forward, such as auto scouting and infinite resource generation sources which makes longer games more viable. 

At the same time, there have been some changes which have felt very regressive. It's somewhat reminiscent of Age of Mythology, both in terms that each major civilization (not counting the cheap variants) play and even advance differently. For the same reason, because of this larger set of mechanics and unit types, they don't appear to be as balanced as they could be. The user interface is also less intuitive and user-friendly than previous instalments, especially when there's such a strong push for a specific style and condensed colour scheme. 

For example: it's pretty impossible to look at the map selection at a glance and tell what's going on. There's black, blue, and gold; the black tends to be land and blue tends to be water, but determining what's going on in the gold pictographs requires some visual scrutiny to interpret, whereas the previous games straight up had close to model mini-maps with full colour, making it instantly clear what was land, resource, cliff and sea.

The building and unit style also somewhat bothers me. Although it's not totally out of character from the 3d models of AOM, this game which was released nineteen years later has an almost Disney-Pixar cartoon-like element that seems to cheapen the atmosphere. Likewise, the campaigns which used to be narrative character-based roleplays are now done from an American History Channel and or BBC infotainment style that both expects their audience to have no attention span or general education, while simultaneously speaking with a delivery that suggests they believe their every sentence will drop your jaw to the floor in wonder.

Ultimately the bottom line is that it doesn't feel fun or engaging to play. I can point to specific things I didn't care for and things that I don't feel measure up to the previous games like I have above, yet I don't think those properly capture why I don't have a good time with this and why even victories don't feel rewarding. I really don't know the reason, so I can only gather that it must be something related to the intrinsically corporate nature of Microsoft vampirising a genre a la Dawn of War III.

206098879.review:
Fun game that is completely ruined by frequent crashing mid-game combined with the lack of a rejoin feature. 2 out of my last 5 games crashed 30-40 minutes into the game, and both would have been wins otherwise. I have a Ryzen 9, 64 gigs of RAM, and a 1660, which should be more than enough to run the game flawlessly, plus I play the game on the lowest graphics settings. I've tried updating graphics drivers, updating motherboard drivers, verifying the integrity of the game files, updating Windows, and optimizing the settings for AOE4 in the Nvidia control panel, but nothing has worked. Some matches run fine, but in others the game will randomly either crash to desktop or blue screen my PC. In the last two matches that crashed, I noticed my opponents had Chinese names (no region lock either apparently), so maybe that had something to do with it, but I have 100 Mbps up and down and should never be having network issues. I submitted a detailed ticket with error logs and my system specs, so we'll see where that goes, but I have very little faith in Microsoft given how awful they are at everything.

Regardless, this game clearly suffers from extremely poor optimization and lacks even the most basic features (rejoin) that should be in nearly every multiplayer game, especially one where matches can last up to an hour. AOE4 is the only game I've played that still finds a way to crash despite every effort I've made to fix the issue.

---{ TEXT }---
Age of Empires IV is part of the most iconic real-time strategy game franchise with a unique and passionately crafted experience that anyone can enjoy. At its core, Age of Empires IV offers the classic formula of resource management, base building, and tactical combat, updated with modern mechanics and smarter AI. The graphics bring detailed battlefields to life with beautiful animations and effects. The soundtrack and sound design remain captivating, capturing the epic scale and intensity of medieval warfare. The game balances history with engaging gameplay, allowing you to lead famous civilizations through pivotal moments in history. In conclusion, this game is a fantastic tribute to a legendary series, combining nostalgia with fresh content and superb gameplay!

---{ 8 / 10 }---

🕹 Review for Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition
💬 Recommended ✅

As a long-time AoE fan, the Anniversary Edition is where Age of Empires IV really hits its stride. The base game was a solid return to classic RTS, but the added civilizations, quality-of-life improvements, and new campaigns take it to the next level.

👍 Pros:

More Civilizations: New civs like the Ottomans and Malians offer fresh, unique playstyles.

Improved UI & Controls: Micromanagement feels smoother, and the updated UI is way more readable.

New Campaigns: The historical storytelling is still top-tier, and the new campaigns are genuinely fun and educational.

Balance Tweaks: The devs have clearly listened to feedback — much better unit and civ balance overall.

Mod Support: Steam Workshop integration is a huge win.

👎 Cons:

AI still meh: It’s better than launch, but still not as smart as you'd hope in single-player skirmishes.

Some visual quirks: Units can still look a bit cartoonish compared to AoE II DE’s art style.

🕰️ Time Played: [Insert your hours here]

If you liked AoE II or III, this is a worthy evolution. It’s not perfect, but the Anniversary Edition shows real polish and passion.

🔥 Final Verdict:
A strong RTS that’s only getting better with time. The Anniversary Edition is what AoE IV should have been at launch.

Age of Empires IV is a good game at its core. The classic RTS foundation is there: gathering resources, building armies, and watching epic medieval battles unfold. The visuals are clean, the music and atmosphere capture the era nicely, and it scratches that nostalgic itch of commanding civilizations through history.

But while it’s fun, it doesn’t always shine. The campaigns feel a bit uneven, the civ variety isn’t as deep as some older titles, and the game can sometimes feel like it’s playing it too safe instead of innovating. Multiplayer is enjoyable but balance issues can creep in.

Overall, AoE4 is solid and definitely worth a try if you love RTS, but it doesn’t quite reach the heights it could. A fun, polished experience — just not groundbreaking.

It really bugs me that there is no Castille or Asturias or any Iberian faction, I've seen people on forums arguing about this, and some say it's because they were not relevant, but the knightly orders that appear in the dlcs were less important on a geopolitical and cultural scale and yet they were swiftly added to the game, and it's been like 3 years since it came out. 

I know the reason though, just like most games in the industry, it only bothers to represent whatever culture the developers were born in, and even if there was a hispanic or iberian, or spanish/portuguese-speaking developer, it just wasn't considered relevant to include it, which may be ok to most people, but I just don't feel motivated to play a game so biased, also it isn't fun to see so many people outright denying the iberians had any history or relevance during the middle ages, that there was no advancements or events relevant or interesting enough to be portrayed in a game such as AoE4. It's infurianting or at least uncomfortable, because not even those who are going to get paid to research history do so, they just go for what's easy, what's going to sell to their local market, but there is more out there, and it's not like I don't care about English or French or Germanic history, I enjoy it when portrayed well, I loved KCD2, I loved AC Unity, I loved previous AoE games, but in a game so big, that should have a more complete focus, that doesn't even need to be THAT complex, I simply expected to see my culture in it, I mean, it was there in AoE3, and although it still was tinted with all that anglosaxon way of seeing us, when I played skirmish for hours and hours on end, I could just forget about that, and it felt good, to feel like I was commanding settlements in my land, like re-living my own history in a way, but now, how am I supposed to do so, how am I supposed to feel connected if I am just not there.

Iberia had and has it all: Knightly orders like the Orden de Santiago (Galicia), Orden de Calatrava (Castilla), Orden de Alcántara (León), and the Orden de Montesa (Aragón), also, the Orden de Cristo (Portugal), and they all fit the time period, portrayed in the game, also thousands of important events and battles to be represented in campaigns, and plenty of kingdoms that were not only relevant within to the Iberian Peninsula, but to Europe and Africa too. As for factions/kingdoms, there was Asturias that became the Kingdom of León, and from which Castilla and Portugal seceded, then in 1230 (within the years portrayed) León and Castilla united, and formed a single kingdom that together with Aragón, Portugal and Navarra pushed the muslims south. So no, Iberian history is not as plain and boring as some people say, and Iberian kingdoms WERE relevant in the middle ages, but if you don't educate yourself on a particular matter, and instead prefer to base your opinion on ambiguous, imprecise, and second-hand information, or worse, your own prejudices, and then tell others, righteously, that THEY are wrong, then I can only advice that you be more humble, if you don't know or don't understand something, be humble, and respect other people's cultures, also don't just rush to do or say stuff if you're not sure of what you know, like, if your knowledge feels like a blurry, cloudy, distant memory or notion, just step back for a while and study. 

History is the memory of the people, it must be cherished, but it must always evolve towards being closer to truth. Truth is fair, and without it we can't do anything, getting closer to it is getting closer to perfection.

I want to finish this letter saying that I love Age of Empires, I've played every single game since I was a child, and I don't think this last entry in the franchise was up to the level of quality of the previous games, at least for what we could expect for the age we live in, perhaps it hasn't been well directed, or maybe it's just trying to follow what's best for profit, and I understand, I know how money works, specially in western countries. I just know my time will be better spent on AoE3 Wars of Liberty or some other game. And it might be just me, but AoE4 seems like a fun and pretty game, but very hollow and lacking as an Age of Empires game.

3/10, wouldn't recommend to any non-anglo/germanic/french person. LOL

Yes, I recommend this game.
I've spent over 90 hours playing Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition, and it's been a deeply rewarding experience for anyone who enjoys real-time strategy and historical immersion. The game successfully blends classic AOE mechanics with modern innovations—like unique civilization designs, landmark systems, and improved visuals.
The Ottoman civilization in particular offers a fresh strategic layer, and the campaigns are not only engaging but educational. Multiplayer and skirmish modes are where the game truly shines, offering intense tactical challenges and solid AI opponents.

Whether you're a casual player or a competitive strategist, this game offers long-term value and replayability. Highly recommended.

204982267.review:
Coming into Age of Empires IV, I couldn’t help but compare it to my childhood favorite, Age of Empires III. That game was my gateway into the RTS genre, and I owe a lot of my love for strategy games to it. So naturally, I was both excited and cautious when I first dove into AoE IV.

The first thing that struck me was how familiar it felt—like coming home to a series I grew up with. The classic formula of building, gathering, and waging war is alive and well, but modernized with smoother controls, polished graphics, and clever quality-of-life improvements. It’s not just nostalgia bait; it feels like a true successor that respects the legacy of the series.

One of the strongest aspects is how differently the civilizations play. From the Mongols’ nomadic playstyle to the English’s defensive might, every faction pushes you to adapt your strategy instead of relying on the same build orders. This makes learning the game a constant adventure, much like discovering Age of Empires III for the first time as a kid.

Another standout is the campaign design. Rather than just giving you missions, Age of Empires IV brings history to life with beautiful documentary-style cutscenes, almost like a playable history channel series. It’s both educational and engaging, and it scratches the same itch that made me fall in love with the franchise years ago.

That said, the game isn’t perfect. At launch, it lacked some of the variety and polish that long-time fans were hoping for, and balancing issues have been a recurring challenge. But the developers have shown strong commitment through updates, and the community around it has been steadily growing.

Overall, Age of Empires IV feels like a bridge between past and present. It may not carry the same personal nostalgia as AoE III—a game tied to my childhood and my father—but it does rekindle that same sense of wonder and excitement. It proves that real-time strategy still has a place in modern gaming, and it carries the torch for a new generation.

Rating (personal): 8.5/10 — not perfect, but a worthy modern heir to the legacy I grew up with.

AGE OF EMPIRES IV: Anniversary Edition

In the series i have only played AoE II, III, and now IV.
AoE II i did not like that one,  it was too difficult and ruthless for my taste. 
One small mistake and forget it! 
you have no chance of winning, might as well restart the mission 
and continue trial and error until you figure out how the developers wants you to solve the mission.
AoE III was one of my all time favourite strategy base building games ever. 
Even thou the story and gameplay sometimes came of a bit childish, with a woman running around getting help from a bear.
This here new chapter AoE IV is my favourite so far!
It has a serious tone in story with historical battles. 
Its not insanly difficult
Which imo is how it should be. 
So those who want to can play on easier difficulty and have a good time just chilling moste of the time.
But when it becomes difficult you can still feel the victory is just around the corner, so it doesnt become frustrating.

203211855.review:
TLDR:   Overall AoE4 is a good game and worth playing if you like RTS games.  

Note for context, the RTS I've played most is Starcraft 2.  The most recent RTS I came from to this one was AoE2 DE.  I've played Iron Harvest, one of the CoH titles, and every Blizzard RTS since WC2 (including Stormgate, if you count that).  Also played a bunch of RTS games back during the during the dawn of time (AoE 1, Total Annihilation, Command and Conquer, etc.)  Here's my thoughts on some comparisons to other RTS games, particularly AoE2...

Unit Pathing and Control:  Unit pathing and control is generally good.  Better than AoE2 and the Company of Heroes type games I've played. It's great when you WANT the units to stay in formation and stick together moving through the field.  It falls apart a bit in two case.  First, you often can't select a bunch of units and order them to just attack somewhere when you want them to just act individually.  They can stay in formation when you don't want.  Second, there's an issue where ranged units especially get stuck attacking buildings.  Other RTS games have often solved this by allowing units to know about enemy units at a farther range than their individual sight range.  


Graphics and Readability:  Graphics are very good for an RTS.  A bit too much focus has been put on visual appeal vs readability, in my opinion.  Part of the issue comes from each unit and building having a different design for each civilization , despite being identical or nearly identical in function.  Some of this may fade with time and experience, but there's just so many variations.  But there;s also things like the 3D nature of your camera and grpahical elements making it not clear exactly where to click.  Other RTS games (including AoE2) have had graphical options which allowed you to better control your experience.


Diversity of Options:  AoE 4 really shines here.  They've done a great job creating many Civilizations which have unique mechanics and units that really make them feel like there's a great variety of play.  And the options within each civ mean that there's almost always more than one way to play it.  They've continued to do this as they add DLC.  Really, this is the strongest selling point of AoE 4.  The Civilizations are not Starcraft-level of different - meaning general skill in the game still translates very well between them - but the civs are far more varied than AoE 2, for example.


AI and Single player:  If you're more of a Campaign-mode player, know that AoE4 does have multiple campaigns.  Some are better than others.  I felt the first one was pretty weak, but it does have to also serve as a tutorial for people who have never player an RTS at all.  Standard matches, AI is decent at the game.  It will give even new players a run for their money at the Hardest setting, particularly if they like to sit back and build eco more.  It's a bit aggressive, with no real option for changing that.  


Some missing features:  Which brings me to one of the big missing features - lack of treaty mode.  Previous incarnations of AoE have always had a "treaty" feature where agression and attacks were banned for some particular time period.  These are some of the most popualr custom games.  They also had options to change the total population you're allowed, etc.  This is missing from AoE4. There are mods you can download to bring these features back in, but the AI is pretty horrible at adjusting to such changes.  And from what I can tell the modding features are farirly limited; enough that you can't really make adjustments to the AI flexibly.

Another glaring missing feature is that the units do NOT show up individually on the command panel. You can see that you have 10 Knights and 5 Pikemen selected, but that takes up 2 squares, not 15. This means you cannot interact with the units from there, or see which ones are injured.  You can't, for example, send All 10 knights to the right and then un-select 5 and send them to the left.  I haven't played an RTS in years that didn't show the units there.

There's a lot of missing features that I would prefer existed, but I can more accept as "design choices" that I wouldn't have made.  You can't zoom out very much.  There's no way to build your buildings so that they are touching and no units can pass between them.  Given the way that units get stuck on buildings more than in oher RTS games, I'd prefer if you had some kind of attack move that didn't attack buildings until the unit reached the space clicked, or just an attack-units command.  I'd really like a "formation" or attack-move type command for my units that told them to move in no formation - that is just attack move at spot X as if I'd issued that command to each unit individually.


Overall it does feel like a modern and fluid RTS.  I pefer playing it to AoE 2, and have generally moved on from AoE2 to this version.  From a purely "competitive RTS" standpoint, I think AoE 2 is a slightly better, but dated game.  AoE 4 probably peaks higher in number of players whenever new DLC comes out, but AoE 2 seems to have a more consitent player base.  

Overall Verdict: AoE 4 is fun game and I do believe I got my money's worth from it.

203167947.review: Age of Empires was amongst my favorite and believed RTS growing up.  Back then, there seemed to be so many more RTS games on the market with Statcraft, Warcraft, Command & Conquer, and many more, but AOE seemed to stick out amongst them.  A Microsoft made game with excellent graphics and gameplay.  Although it plays like a great RTS with a fun campaign, opening it and the overall UI felt like you were experiencing and looking at a school program encyclopedia that had a built in RTS game as an option.  The multiplayer is definitely fun, but I’m just a casual player and there are definitely sweatys in this community that all have almost the same strategies.  I enjoy the historical aspect of it and like to build out a city and go through the different time periods.  When you play multiplayer you definitely have to play differently than you would in a single player match.  The campaign is really well done.  Microsoft definitely made a great experience with Age of Empires in how it’ll walk you through the basics and what not.  If you’re a fan of the AOE franchise I think this polished up version of the game is a welcomed addition.  Fans of RTS may enjoy it, but might prefer something more fast paced or action oriented like a StarCraft.  Classic PC gamers in general I think would enjoy it.  Not exactly a game you can quickly jump in and out of as you could easily sink a couple hours into a match.  Still recommend especially if you can get it on sale.

Okay so I am an OG Player. I play since AOE 1 early versions before the sound editing and censorships.

I finished every AOE game out there except Definitive editions, stopped there. I would like to say that this is exactly what this series needed. AOE 4 is revolutionary in the RTS genre and I am happy to see Relic creating good rts games again! 

For me something very important in all AOE games was the Single Player Campaigns. I was disappointed when they stopped narrating the stories. I loved the AOE 2 narrations (briefing screen before each mission) and the art as well. Small things but still important to me.

They went above and beyond in this category with AOE 4 as the movies and historical facts are on point! If you know your history you know what I mean. 

The multiplayer is also brilliant no more desync, no more infernal LAG! The balancing is decent if they make mistakes like releasing a bit too overpowered faction they will balance/fix it pretty fast. 

Snowflakes complain about DLCS. I mean they are usually 15-20$ for factions that are useful for multiplayer (COH players know this system very well) and I think it is okay the way it is!

203002300.review:
Excessive macro mechanics have ruined this game. Even different sub-factions within the same civilization possess entirely distinct technologies, units, and opening strategies. For players, it means an overwhelming learning curve, while for the development team, it means an almost impossible task of achieving mechanical balance. 

The sub-faction models in Company of Heroes 1 and Command & Conquer: Generals Zero Hour serve as benchmarks, perfectly demonstrating how to deliver rich variety through faction specialties under 3-5 sets of macro mechanics. However, Relic has clearly deviated from this approach. 

Game design is not just about complex mechanisms; knowing how to simplify and refine is what truly distinguishes exceptional designers.

202946690.review:
This game has serious matchmaking issues that ruin the competitive experience. It constantly mixes Xbox players with PC players, which creates an uneven playing field. On top of that, the system often throws players into ranked matches who clearly don’t belong in that tier.

Once you hit Platinum or higher, things get even worse. You’ll frequently be matched with teammates far below your rank, and early leavers are common, leaving you in hopeless situations. Unless you queue with friends, the experience is miserable.

Competitive games should reward skill and provide fair matchups, but this game fails to deliver on both fronts. Until the developers fix these core problems, I can’t recommend it for anyone looking for a balanced, enjoyable ranked experience.

202817032.review: I'm a decades long Age of Empires fan but this game is BRUTAL!  (in a good way).  My first evening with the game was spent regretting my life choices as the AI handed my ass to me on Moderate difficulty.  It's a whole different beast and you really have to learn to keep those villagers and trade caravans spawning whilst also utilising multiple military buildings simultaneously.  The story campaigns are very well designed with amazing historical mini documentaries as rewards which are fascinating.  By day two I can beat the AI on moderate (just) and it was SO satisfying to see their town in ruins, words just can't convey my sense of achievement.  It's a modern take on the formula which I'm really enjoying and the amount of content available is pretty impressive.  I'd give it a 8/10 GREAT rating, it's well recommended.

202813996.review:
[b]Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition[/b] is a visually stunning and strategically rich RTS that effectively revitalizes the franchise. If you value polished historical campaigns, diverse civs, and refined multiplayer—especially with controller support—you’ll find a lot to love.

This is the most enjoyable Age of Empires experience to date! The visuals are stunning with bright colors and epic environments. It also showcases subtle yet impactful nuances of your units; pulling out torches to set enemy buildings on fire and even vivid cloud reflections on the rivers. There is an assortment of gameplay between the different civilizations ranging from European, Asian and African, even requiring different skill levels to master the differences.

Offers four campaigns spanning hundreds of years, enhanced with documentary-style video segments that bring real-world context to gameplay. Eight unique civs with varied strategies—like mobile Mongol camps or Rus hunting bonuses—add depth and replayability. Simplified mechanics lower the barrier to entry for newcomers while retaining core strategic depth. Narrated sequences and historical touches enrich the narrative, making the campaigns more than just battles. Allows both unit control and broader strategy to shine.

[b]Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition[/b] brings back the classic RTS experience with accessible design, distinctive civilizations, and engaging narrative campaigns. It's a reliable and polished entry that bridges nostalgia with modern sensibilities—though it plays it relatively safe in terms of innovation. If you're into historical strategy, compelling civ variety, and approachable gameplay, it’s a strong pick. Longtime fans hoping for groundbreaking changes, however, might find it comfortably familiar rather than revolutionary.

[h1]9/10[/h1]
DISASTER | BAD | MEDIOCRE | OKAY | GOOD | GREAT | [b][u]AMAZING [/u][/b]| MASTERPIECE

202774786.review:
Let's do a quick verdict after about an hour into the game. 

Negatives: 
This game has no blood or wounds or damaged armor and the like. It feels like you're playing with dolls.
Controlling things in this game is a chore. How do I deselect 1 unit from a group, say to bring it to the back? Why do I have to go in and change every single option such as for cycling units, selecting archery ranges and everything. They should already be done by the devs, instead, those hotkeys are preselected with random junk almost no one uses. When I set up a rally point to a tree from the TC, why must I constantly change it when the tree gets chopped, they should simply go to the next available tree. Why can't I see the HP bar of the units in a formation? Why can't I change behaviour of the units such as aggressive/defensive? Why don't units have portraits like in other Age games or RTS? I want my big buff guy since AoE1. This game feels like the Total War games where individual units don't matter, it's unlike the other Age games.

Neutral:
Since this is a documentary type of campaigns, it means that the devs are more limited with their imagination. Something I like about Aoe2 campaigns is that sometimes when we don't have enough information about specific events, the devs can throw in interesting side quests or campaign missions or exxagerate what happened for dramatic effect. If they do it well, it can immerse you in the role of say being a conquerer or a defender against a specific conquest.

Positives: 
The documentary videos are really well done. It's like you're watching a documentary on your PC with some gameplay thrown in between. Great narration, camera work, sound and visuals, just excellent. Gold is more useful throughout the game, it's used even for economic upgrades, it encourages people to diversify their economy. 

My big big problems with the games are the controls and that it doesn't feel like an Age of Empires game. It feels like I'm playing some big army simulation, which Age games can be but that's much later than usual, in most games of say AoE2, you kind of have to protect your units, to do micro and the like until the very end when you're maxed out and you can send units to their deaths no problemo. I like that the devs encourage the use of gold earlier, you can also speed up aging up by adding more villagers to build the building. But overall, the experience is too different from the other games in the series. Not to my liking. If you're into big simulation games with some base building, this is the game for you. If you're an AoE fan, this one may be too different from the rest.

202524573.review:
I really loved this game at launch. I still think it has amazing graphics and sound design, and the best core gameplay of the entire Age series. But the developers have completely lost the plot on managing it post launch. All we get are over-priced DLC that deliver nothing but weirdly niche "variant" civilizations. 

This franchise is supposed to be about empires and cultures, but all we get are individual armies and noble houses. AoE is supposed to be about the history of the whole world, but all we get are more European and Asian stuff. Years since launch and there's still just one African civilization and nothing from the Americas. But hey, at least we're getting a second Japanese civilization to please the weebs, right?

 Campaigns have been abandoned. The skirmish AI has been broken since launch. AoE2 thrives because it supports casuals and single-player fans as much as competitive players, but AoE4 only cares about ladder sweatlords.

This doesn't feel like an Age of Empires game anymore. It's lost the soul of what the franchise great.

201836887.review:
I've played every single AoE game and has been a lifelong fan. It pains me to say this, but I can't recommend this game. The campaigns are hideously boring and sterile, especially compared to AoE II. Macro is worse than AoE III. Unit control and upgrading units and buildings are worse than both. No Native American civs. Only one African Civ is represented (Malians). Graphics is passable, but unimpressive. And worst of all, NO HISTORY SECTION!!!

I don't like how Stand Ground command keeps toggling off whenever you do anything with a unit. Unlike SCII, your units don't seem to have target priority, so they'll always run off chasing a villager to get themselves killed, or are busy shooting at a house behind them while getting pasted by enemy fire. 201675106.review:
This game is incomplete and a money grab.  Do not buy.

Pros

1. Good Graphics
2. Built in fixes for many irritants of Age of Empires 2.
3. They combined many of the best elements of AOE2 and AOE3 into a decent looking game.  This is what we expected from the game makers when AOE3 came out.
4. Units are a little smarter than AOE2 ones, but not by much
5. Ability for units to go around structures and not get stuck.

Negatives: 
1. The art style for the menu and unit menus are lazy and unimaginative.  If they are looking to copy AOE 2 and make it better, then they should have used a nicer looking AOE 2 style options for units and menus vs the shiny gold look that they used.  It is just plane tacky.
2. Unit control setup is similar enough to what gamers are used to, but far clutzier than the original.  Example, you can select your units to attack a specific structure, but not a specific unit . . .  Unless the game decides to let you for some odd reason.
3. Features and styles that came as standard in AOE, AOE2, and AOE3 were removed.  AKA they removed the map maker.  This individual tool is what made the older games so re-playable.  I know that the game makers have the software on their end, but the fact that they removed it speaks volumes about how shady they are becoming.  They do not want competition for beautiful maps or campaigns.  

I can live with annoying art styles and unit controls.  It is just something to get used to.  When game makers remove a standard feature that has been in all their games up to this point, this is completely unacceptable.  I do not plan on playing this game often in the future until they add the map maker back to the game.

The good:

its a good quality RTS game with alot of complexity and strategy. just like its predecessors, its fun and engaging. there is no story aside from historical battles(which is a shame, i was hoping for another good piece of historical fiction), so starting from here is not a problem.

The bad:

the game is missing an insane amount of features when compared to its predecessors. i was very disappointed that my options were so limited. the enemy AI is horrendously bad, again, especially when compared to predecessors.

the ugly:

provided i pay them extra money, i can play 3 different versions of the English, 2 different versions of the french, 2 different versions of the Abbasid, and 2 different versions of the Romans, but the Spaniards, vikings, and an insane amount of other civilizations don't have a playable faction. these DLC look more like asset flips than additional content. 

the verdict:

200378991.review:
I feel the same as a few commenters and critics here. 

The game is so nice visually. It's a real feast, and the glossy documentaries that accompany the main game are almost worth the price of admission. I cannot thank the team enough for them. They feel like the kind of stuff we used to get back in the 2000s, only with fun, interactive elements. 

While this game has a lot of polish, the game is severely lacking in the single player department. Campaign missions are combat orientated, so much so it under-tutorialises about late game economies, and the new mechanics you have to play with. The mad scramble for wood is pretty much gone, and so you have to unlearn a lot, refocusing on relic control and creating smarter, smaller farms. 

The enemy AI fails to start in some matches, especially matches with an unconventional map. Black forest is unplayable, as around half the games I played involve the enemy AI rushing to Fuedal, only to stop dead for some reason. On easy, it's as much as ⅔s. Even on a purely random map, if there is a pond nearby the enemy AI will fill it with boats, even at the expense of  an enemy on their doorstep. You have to crank the enemy all the way up to hard, and even then they tend to favour their special units. This is a disaster for the Chinese, as you will start to fight wave after wave of repeating crossbows and battering rams that are only really good at bullying villagers. 

All enemy AI struggles when you build up nearby. You can effectively freeze them in feudal just by building a palisade fort nearby. They will waste wave after wave, and all difficulty increases do is make the faux-micro look more tight.

Multiplayer is okay, but it lacks the snappiness that made AoE2 so satisfying. Formations don’t swing into position, they fumble past each other, and god help you if you wanna play a naval map. It's a micro nightmare, like watching elephants stumble over each other. I was able to win just by hanging back and watching the enemy try to get their ducks in a row. It reminds me, in a bad way, of playing DoW back in the day, only lacking the visual flourishes that made that fun.

Normally I would advise waiting, but the devs don’t seem to be keen on fixing the game. DLC is slow, and the latest was a disaster; effectively paying for a mod. It feels like they watched T90, took notes, and not really listened to what the rest of the playerbase wants. Give me enemy AI that feels interesting, maybe allow me to control aspects of behaviour, or the ability to handicap the enemy and myself.

200270490.review:
A Treatise on the Strategic Cataclysm That Is Age of Empires IV
Or, How I Lost My Will to Micromanage While My Friends Evolved Into Tyrannical Macro Gods

It is with great anguish and scholarly exasperation that I draft this extensive lamentation, a negative—nay, despair-laden—review of Age of Empires IV, a game whose design complexity could only have been forged in the hidden archives of Byzantine war councils or the fever dreams of a caffeine-fueled chess grandmaster with a grudge against casual players.

Let us begin by addressing the learning curve—a term which here operates not as a metaphor, but rather as an active threat. The curve is not a curve; it is a sheer cliff face coated in grease and lined with boiling oil, manned by the smug avatars of your two overachieving friends. While I attempted to shepherd my hapless villagers into some semblance of economic productivity, they were already fielding siege workshops, implementing precise hotkey rotations, and orchestrating synchronized multi-pronged cavalry raids like digital Napoleons. I, on the other hand, was staring at my lumber camp wondering why my villagers kept idling like they were on break at a Renaissance Starbucks.

The game claims accessibility, but this is a facade more deceptive than a Mongol ambush cloaked in fog of war. The tutorial lovingly teaches you how to click a sheep and build a house, and then promptly releases you into a match where your opponent’s APM (actions per minute) would shame a hummingbird on Red Bull. No mention is made of the importance of resource balancing with monk-supported relic control, or the sinister meta involving proxy stables and dark age tower rushes. I went in with dreams of historical conquest and came out with PTSD from a trebuchet barrage at minute twelve.

Do not be misled by the polished user interface and the dulcet tones of the narrator explaining medieval architecture. Behind this charming veil lies an infernal symphony of resource optimization, tech tree memorization, scouting patterns, and precise build orders—each more essential than the last. Failure to mine gold at the correct velocity? Prepare to be speared into oblivion. Forget to build a blacksmith by the time the moon reaches its zenith? Say hello to a wave of men-at-arms with +1 attack upgrades smashing your barracks like it owes them money.

And then there's the sheer indecipherable chaos of combat. Units clash in clumps so tangled that trying to select anything is like performing surgery in the dark while being screamed at by your dying economy. Micro one knight to kite? Cool, now your entire wood line is on fire. The game punishes every lapse in attention like a strict Jesuit headmaster with a passion for punitive economics.

In our match—if one can call it a match and not an anthropological reenactment of the conquest of the Inca—I watched in horror as my one friend’s holy Roman empire bulldozed through our meager defense with the terrifying inevitability of entropy. Their prelates chanted. Their villagers prospered. My town center, meanwhile, was converted before I could even queue a spearman. My other ally was too busy being eviscerated by camel archers to help.

In conclusion, Age of Empires IV is not a real-time strategy game. It is a doctoral thesis in time management, resource economics, and psychological warfare disguised as a game. It punishes the unprepared, humiliates the hopeful, and rewards only those who have spent more hours studying armor counters than they did for their SATs. I came to build an empire. I left with a profound sense of inadequacy and a burning desire to uninstall.

Rating: 2/10, only because the sheep are cute.

The game itself is a real masterpiece and the campaign is mindblowingly good... BUT I have two significant call outs that really need improvement and tarnish the overall experience of this game.

1) The UI before you get into a game is so incredibly woeful, like literally the worst, I've ever seen in any RTS game I've ever played. After all these years, its still buggy and not at all intuitive, doesn't display icons properly, the friend system leaves much to be desired and is missing a bunch of key quality of life settings, it derps out when trying to find team games where a player doesn't fully join a lobby.

2) The second major frustration is team games. Basically when you're playing any kind of team game, 2v2, 3v3, or 4v4, and someone leaves (which happens painfully often), the game is essentially ruined. Why they don't have the AI take over or let the remaining players control what's left (with slit resources) is beyond me. They could learn a thing or two from Starcraft 2, which is significantly older than AOE4.

⭐ Age of Empires IV — A Strong Return to Form
AoE IV brings back classic RTS gameplay with a modern touch. Unique civs, historical campaigns, and solid mechanics make it a great experience for both veterans and newcomers.

👍 What’s Great:

Distinct civilizations with unique mechanics

High-quality campaigns with docu-style storytelling

Polished tutorials and accessible design

Lots of replayability and strategic depth

👎 Needs Work:

Some missing features (scenario editor, deeper hotkey options)

Pathfinding and AI can still be clunky

Visuals feel a bit dated; limited camera zoom

Verdict:
A solid revival that nails the basics and offers hours of fun. It’s not perfect, but it’s definitely worth your time—especially if you love strategy.

199687096.review:
A Solid Revival of a Classic RTS
Age of Empires IV brings back the nostalgic RTS feel of AoE II, but with a modern engine and fresh historical campaigns. It sticks to what made the series great—strong economy management, tech trees, base building, and epic large-scale battles—while adding new layers of civilization-specific mechanics and polished visuals.

Pros:
⚔️ Classic AoE gameplay with smoother mechanics

🏰 Civilizations feel distinct and well-balanced

📚 Campaigns are rich, educational, and immersive (with real history videos!)

🎨 Updated graphics and crisp sound design

🌐 Multiplayer is active and constantly evolving

Cons:
👥 Limited civs at launch (expansions help this)

🧭 Some UI elements and hotkeys could be better

🤖 AI and pathfinding still need work in tight battle areas

Verdict:
If you're an AoE II veteran or an RTS fan looking for something that respects the classics while stepping into the modern age, AoE IV is absolutely worth playing. It’s not perfect, but it’s grown a lot since launch and is now a strong contender in the RTS space.

🟢 Would recommend, especially with friends or if you're into historical campaigns.

199122265.review:
I really wanted to love this game, but I can’t. I have really enjoyed playing AOE III Definitive Edition and Age of Mythology Retold. These are great games with solid graphics, enemy AI, and tons of customization to allow you to make the game what you want it to be. Sadly, this has none of that. The graphics are good if you accept the extremely limited camera angles they give you. I like the freedom to view the game from any angle so I did add in the free camera mod, which gives you the ability to zoom in or out as far as you want, rotate 360 degrees, etc. However, after using this mod I found that the graphics are very poor compared to AOE III DE and Age of Mythology Retold. If you zoom out (not even that far) everything disappears into a white cloud. The distance rendering is straight up awful. 
I really like to play Skirmish battles, but somehow, they even got this wrong. The number of maps is very limited, and the computer AI is awful compared to other AOE games. The computer-built towns are ugly, inefficient, and prevent their own units from moving around the map. The AI’s armies move illogically (yes, I have tried all the different difficulties and I don’t think the computer gets that much better on the higher difficulties), and the computer's only tactic is to swarm you. For some reason, they also left out many of the diplomacy and ally command abilities from the other games, again limiting your approach to play/win.
This could be a great game. I love the time period and civilization options. If they would just upgrade it with the same features as AOE III DE or AOM-R this game would be stellar. Sadly, as is I would pass. I tried to fix the games shortcomings with Mods, but even they were not enough. I also think it's sad that a game that has been out for years has so many. shortcomings still present. It feels like they made half a game and said, “well, the modders will finish it”.

198945452.review:
Gameplay Mechanics: 8/10
Narrative: 7/10
Graphics: 8/10
Sound and Music: 8/10
Replayability: 8/10
Innovation: 7/10

Age of Empires IV is a return to form for one of the most iconic RTS franchises. It blends the classic mechanics that fans love with a modern coat of paint and some thoughtful new additions. While it doesn’t completely reinvent the genre, it definitely brings it back into the spotlight.

Gameplay mechanics are tight and familiar. Base-building, resource gathering, and tactical army management are as satisfying as ever. Each civilization feels unique, with meaningful differences in playstyle—especially the Mongols and Delhi Sultanate. It plays like a love letter to Age of Empires II, with some new mechanics layered in. Score: 8/10.

Narrative is delivered in an interesting documentary-style format during the campaign. It’s educational and polished, offering context and history for each mission. While it doesn’t offer a traditional story arc, it’s a unique approach that works surprisingly well. Score: 7/10.

Graphics are clean and vibrant. Units are well-animated, the environments are rich, and performance is solid. Some fans wished for a grittier tone, but the art direction is clear and readable, which is great for gameplay clarity. Score: 8/10.

Sound and music are excellent. Each civilization has its own flavor, and the soundtrack dynamically evolves with your progress. Voiceovers and battle sounds are immersive. Score: 8/10.

Replayability is strong. With multiple civs, PvP, skirmishes, and mods, there’s plenty to keep RTS fans engaged long after the campaign ends. Score: 8/10.

Innovation is modest. While the asymmetrical civ designs are a nice touch, the game plays it fairly safe. It’s more of a refined revival than a groundbreaking evolution. Score: 7/10.

Bottom line: Age of Empires IV doesn’t shake up the RTS formula, but it polishes and celebrates it. If you’ve missed the golden age of strategy games, this one brings that magic back with style and substance.

198786017.review:
Age of Empires IV is a return to a more classic AoE formula from AoE 3, a game that took the series in a different direction to somewhat polarizing results (at least, that's what I remember at the time, I could just be misremembering). In theory, that could be a good thing- I think Age of Empires 2 is the most "classic" Age of Empires experience, and I have fond memories growing up of playing all kinds of community maps and running through the campaigns of Age of Empires 2. 

What Age of Empires IV, in theory, aims to achieve is a grand return to form, and I think to some extent it does... but in other ways it doesn't. The graphical updates are actually very strong, and while it's not a graphical powerhouse it has an aesthetic and direction that is both inoffensive to people who enjoy the franchise and newcomers alike, while being generally appealing. The gameplay loop returns to a more classic formula, with a few innovations that make sense and work well. The community features are now much more readily integrated into the game through an in game community mod and map browser. However, these feel somewhat mediocre in light of how the game has aged, or failed to do so. 

What do I mean by that? Well, if you look at my playtime, I obviously have some very strong opinions for someone who has played relatively little on Steam, and that's because I played AoE4 at launch on GamePass. I enjoyed it, but found the amount of content somewhat lacking and at the time I felt there was a need for polish. And while some of the roughest edges did get addressed, AoE IV still has the problem of being the weakest entry in the series in terms of width. With only ten civilizations in the Anniversary Edition (eight at launch) and the removal of team synergies (something that added depth to team compositions in AoE 2 and was part of the deck building in AoE 3) there just isn't a lot of content compared to previous entries, and while the civilizations are well done and somewhat more diverse than in AoE 2 what it does feel like is that some of the more interesting factions are locked behind DLC.

Which is ultimately one of the reasons why this is a not recommended. In a trend that's increasingly common in modern games, it does feel like a good portion of the game's content, including some pretty basic map types, civilizations (though some of these are just variants, so not even entirely new civilizations), and campaigns (which at least make sense) are locked behind DLC in a way that feels like the game was designed to be trickled. While I'm not necessarily defending the other games in the franchise (AoE 3 also launched with eight civilizations, for example, though the DLC added some really transformative and unique civilizations instead of just variants) it's just hard to recommend Age of Empires IV on Steam in the context of that detail. For $40 on Steam, vs. AoE 3 DE that has more content for $20, AoE 2 DE has much more content for $35, and while they both have issues with DLC of varying value I don't think AoE 4 AE brings competitive value.

Part of the problem is that AoE 4 AE just has some issues that don't land. For all the attention to detail and coolness of the updates, villagers will clip through your town center- for a game designed to sell the immersion of your development through things like adding environmental feelies as you add more houses in an area, not setting a bounding box a little further out is an oversight. The rock-paper-scissors gameplay is still here, but after the various mercenaries and map villages you can enlist in AoE3 the roster feels small for some civilizations. It feels like a game that has been intentionally narrowed in scope, and that's not an entirely bad thing, but it does mean that the gameplay loop is not as wide or meaningful. 

I don't think AoE 4 is a bad game, but rather I just don't think it's worth the price tag in 2025- for what is essentially $70 to get the "full" game, which unfortunately is not an unusual price tag in 2025, you could pick up multiple other games including other AoE games that have stood the test of time better (if you don't care about graphics). I wasn't impressed with how the game evolved over time, and while the Definitive Editions of the other AoE games have their own problems with DLC I think it's fair to say that if you're here for nostalgia, it would be better to go to those instead. That said, the RTS genre is so dead as of late that if you are just looking for an RTS game you could do worse than AoE 4, just know that it will be holding out a hand for more money if you don't buy all the DLC as you scratch your head about why a slightly different variant of a map is locked behind a paywall.

The good part:
/ City Building mechanics that make every faction have a specific plan to it.
/ UNIQUE linguistic, aesthetic and architectural changes every time you enter a new age, in context at the period, for all civs
/ Battle mechanics feel dynamic and alive
/ Music and sound design is amazing
/ Continuous improvements and support done by the devs that bring more historical depth and more things to try out
/ Single-player/PvE replayability feels good - no game feels the same.

The bad part:
x Devs cater to multiplayer side too much, that it sacrificed scale over convenience (e.g. micro-Hippodrome lol)
x Battles are too fast paced that you don't have time to enjoy the battles.
x Naval gameplay REALLY NEEDS an overhaul (ship battles need different principles and approaches for its gameplay)
x Sieges are too fast - Walls are way too weak to hold.
x AI doesn't wall-in sometimes

Parts that need improvement:
Some of the stuff I want is just for single player, campaign, Custom games, and PvE as an option
- Battle and siege pacing need to slow down a bit
- Some landmarks need better scaling
- Rotate buildings for city building
- another sandbox mode, but for city-builders: doing sandbox alone on maps will generate resources for only 1 player.
- Better modability: Tuning pack only uses 1 mod. Other RTS games don't do this kind of restriction, hence they have more replayability.

197934509.review:
Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition celebrates one of the most iconic real-time strategy franchises with a polished, expanded, and lovingly crafted experience that both longtime fans and newcomers can enjoy. This edition not only refines the base game’s strengths but also adds fresh content and quality-of-life improvements that deepen the strategic gameplay.

At its core, Age of Empires IV offers the classic RTS formula of resource management, base building, and tactical combat, updated with modern mechanics and smarter AI. The Anniversary Edition introduces new civilizations, units, and campaigns that enrich the strategic variety and replayability.

The game balances historical authenticity with engaging gameplay, allowing you to lead famous civilizations through pivotal moments in history with distinct playstyles and unique abilities.

The Anniversary Edition shines with expanded campaigns that are meticulously designed to tell compelling stories through challenging missions. The new content offers deeper insights into historical events, enhanced with detailed narration, cutscenes, and dynamic objectives that keep the gameplay fresh.

The updated graphics bring vibrant, detailed battlefields to life with improved animations and environmental effects. The soundtrack and sound design remain immersive, capturing the epic scale and intensity of medieval warfare.

Multiplayer remains a cornerstone, with robust matchmaking, ranked play, and custom game options. The Anniversary Edition improves stability and introduces new features to enhance the competitive scene, fostering a thriving community.

Pros:
-Classic RTS gameplay polished with modern improvements
-Expanded content with new civilizations and campaigns
-Stunning visuals and immersive sound design
-Improved multiplayer features and community support
-Balanced gameplay appealing to both casual and hardcore players

Cons:
-Steeper learning curve for newcomers to the series
-Some balance issues remain between certain civilizations
-Campaign pacing can feel uneven at times

Age of Empires IV: Anniversary Edition is a fantastic tribute to a legendary series, blending nostalgia with fresh content and refined gameplay. Whether you’re diving into the campaigns or battling friends online, this edition delivers a rich, rewarding RTS experience that stands the test of time.

Rating: 8/10

196364683.review:
I fell in love with this franchise almost 20 years ago, when I played the early installments. I've just stumbled upon this latest release and I can't put it down!

The Single Player is solid and offers various modes, including Campaign, Skirmish, and Proving Grounds. For history enthusiasts, I strongly recommend purchasing the The Sultans Ascend expansion to play the campaign of the same name. Truly thrilling! For those who enjoy tougher challenges, you’ll definitely get your money’s worth with the new four scenarios in the Proving Grounds featured in the Knights of Cross and Rose expansion. I’m curious to see how you’ll fare in Conqueror mode!

Multiplayer is just as fun and challenging, both in 1v1 and Team vs Team modes. Each season offers new rewards, and the maps you battle on are regularly updated.

The only drawbacks I’d mention are the AI in Skirmish mode, which could use some improvement, and the banner customization - I’d love to see more options there. Lately, matchmaking has also seemed to take a slight dip in quality, but I’m confident the developers will find a way to fix it. The development team is clearly investing heavily in this title, having promised another DLC coming out later this year (2025).

195701082.review:
After a long break, I decide to start a skirmish game against an intermediate AI on the black forest map. After a while, I notice that the AI is doing almost nothing. All its villagers are on idle except for one fisherman, and its cavalry is just randomly running around in its base. I attack the AI and steamroll it completely. The victory grants me the achievement for beating a hard and hardest AI (again, my opponent was set to intermediate). After a brief internet search, it seems that the "AI doing nothing"-bug has existed for at least 2 years already.

It pains me to give this game a negative review. AoE2 was my first PC game and I played the ever living hell out of it as a kid. I would've loved to see the series move forward, and AoE4 seemed like it had the potential to do so substantially. But as it stands now, it's just an inferior buggy mess.

195659967.review:
I really like strategy games, but for a long time I felt like RTS is too stressful. I internalised some esports truisms about APM and reaction speed, and AoE4 is just not that game! It's fundamentally about economy, information, and decision-making - unit counters are really strong, so if you scout well and build your army accordingly, you'll win fights with attack-move even if your opponent micros like crazy. See them building a barracks in the Dark Age? Better get a tower up. They start building a second town center? Dial up the aggression and punish their greed. It's really rewarding!

When I win, it's because I start with a plan and adapt to what my opponent's doing on the fly. When I lose, it's because I don't know what's going on and run my spearmen into an archer firing squad while my opponent slurps up all the delicious resources on the map and I panic-build farms.

Very crude guide to success:
1) train villagers constantly to grow your economy (seriously, you want like a hundred villagers)
2) keep your villagers busy, gathering the right mix of resources to support your plan
3) spend the resources on buildings and military units
4) adapt to what your opponent is doing (defend against rushes, punish greed, counter their army comp, etc)

Anyway, if you like the premise of RTS games but feel like they're not approachable enough for your middle-aged gamer sensibilities, I really encourage you to give AoE4 a try. It's been very rewarding, and even in very close, stressful games, I don't feel that exhaustion after a game that I often have in other competitive multiplayer games.

195646734.review: Terrible balance. There's absolutely no point in playing defensively in this game. A difference of just one or two upgrades turns an upgraded unit into a terminator that your whole army can shoot at, and its health will decrease extremely slowly. Awful matchmaking in ranked mode — if you're a beginner, you'll be thrown against players who have been playing for a long time. All in all, I wasted my money.

195603622.review:
I honestly don't understand why this game didn't blow up more. Maybe it's because people are losing interest in history these days, or maybe they just don't have the patience for the slower, methodical gameplay that RTS games demand - you know, the careful city building, the gradual army development, all that strategic planning that takes time to pay off.
As someone who grew up on the entire Age of Empires series, plus Rise of Nations and Age of Mythology.
The graphics are a huge step up from the previous games, and the controls and UI feel so much more polished.
What really gets me is the attention to detail - those tiny animations of villagers going about their work, the little flourishes that bring the world to life. It's incredibly satisfying just to watch your civilization bustling with activity.
The gameplay itself is solid RTS territory - commanding armies, plotting strategic moves. But what I really appreciate is how the game gives you room to recover from setbacks. Even when enemies hit you hard, you can adapt your strategy, reinforce your defenses, and completely turn the tide of battle. I like that kind of challenge.
My only real complaint is that the campaigns could use more historical depth. There are so many fascinating wars and conflicts throughout history.
Overall, it's a great game that deserves more attention than it got.

195231949.review:
As someone who spent their childhood listening to the soothing click of villagers chopping wood and building little houses (yes, Age of Empires 2, I’m looking at you), I was a bit skeptical about AoE IV at first. But… the Anniversary Edition pleasantly surprised me.

First off – the graphics. Everything looks like someone took a medieval diorama, sprinkled some historical vibes on it, and cranked it up to 4K. It's clean, crisp, and most importantly – you can actually see what's going on. No more guessing whether that’s a farmer or a mustached spy.

The sounds? Catapults go BOOM, arrows whizz past, and soldiers yell things I don’t understand, but they sound mad enough that I believe them.

The biggest win, though, is in the campaigns and civilizations. Each civ has its own flavor and unique tricks, and if you pick the Mongols, you’ll discover that building a base on wheels isn’t just a caravaner’s dream – it’s a legit strategy. The campaigns feel like a BBC documentary… but with the option to unleash medieval chaos instead of just watching it.

As for gameplay – it’s classic AoE, but with a modern coat of paint. No insane micro like Starcraft, but still enough depth for a 14-year-old with lightning-click reflexes to crush your dreams. And multiplayer? Forget reality, just drop a castle next to your opponent’s base and watch panic ensue.

The Anniversary Edition adds a ton – new civs, better balance, improved tutorials (yes, even us veterans need refreshers), and the sense that the game truly respects its roots.
